I tried posting to OnlyFans for 30 days and it turned out to be the best side hustle discovery that I've made so far. I put everything on the line all to find out how much money can you make from OnlyFans in 30 days with no experience? The OnlyFans platform never says that it's for adult content, but everyone knows it's for adult content. You upload photos and videos of yourself and then people pay a monthly price to get access to that and they can even send you tips to throw more money at you for no reason. and ended up reaching the top 3% of earners on the entire platform. My thought process with the whole experiment was that people can see content for free whenever they want. The reason someone would pay for adult content is to see someone specific and in this case I guess, me, I don't know, I don't know why. Apparently that's fairly high, but I'm worth it. Just kidding. But no, it is $22 a month. On day one, I had just a couple of photos and videos uploaded and I made $52.80 from three new fans. Even though that's a bunch of money, earnings had slowed a bit compared to the end of week two and that's when I realized that the earnings seemed to correlate with how many views I was getting on YouTube. When my YouTube videos got more views more people came across my OnlyFans link which is in all the video descriptions and more people went and subscribed. I wanted to make my OnlyFans earnings less dependent on YouTube views, so I turned to TikTok. The problem was that TikTok doesn't let you advertise an OnlyFans account directly. Even though OnlyFans isn't specifically for adult content, TikTok knows what people use it for. I run my first TikTok ad for two days and I spent $304 on it. That like immediately got me back $278 from subscriptions and tips. So basically I spent $2 acquiring each new fan and some of those will go on to spend more money with me in the future through tips and re-subscriptions. I turned off the ad because I want to spend some time tweaking it a bit more to try to make it profitable but I think this TikTok ad setup is a bit like a cheat code for OnlyFans. By the end of the 30 day experiment I'd made a grand total of $1,628 but I had no idea if anyone who'd subscribed would end up renewing their subscription. Maybe everyone had just subscribed for one month and then not bother sticking around. There was only one way to find out, keep going for another month. OnlyFns and Audience Interactions (0:04 - 3:31):*
Ankita discusses the nature of her content, noting that while some creators earn significantly, it's often misrepresented in India (0:04-0:17).
She shares bizarre requests she has received, including a fan who paid 35,000 INR just to be yelled at, which she initially hesitated to do (0:21-1:01).
Her subscription and call packages are structured to keep things exclusive; video calls are priced at 30,000+ INR to discourage non-serious users (1:11-1:32).
Beyond the explicit, she encounters fans who come to her for emotional support or to talk about personal crises, like depression or marital issues (2:17-3:31).

Social Media, Influence, and Public Perception (4:06 - 7:07):
She discusses the danger of stalkers and abusive messages, noting she handles them by exposing the offenders on her stories (4:06-4:28).
When critics label her content as a "bad influence" on children, she argues that she is not a teacher, and that the audience should monitor what their children watch (4:46-5:05).
She highlights that her success comes from hard work and brand deals, and she refuses to apologize for the persona she presents online (6:16-7:05).

Celebrity Attention and Relationships (7:51 - 13:42):
Ankita mentions receiving DMs from various celebrities and cricketers, which she largely ignores to maintain professionalism (7:51-8:50).
She addresses her public image, noting that people often assume she parties excessively, despite her being relatively introverted (9:04-9:10).
She has been in a committed relationship for seven years and plans to marry; her partner and family are supportive and understand the difference between her online persona and her real life (11:30-11:58).

Financial Goals and Future Outlook (13:13 - 21:02):
Ankita manages her finances through her father and has invested in real estate, including properties in Noida and Agra (13:47-13:55).
She expresses a strong desire to transition into mainstream acting, noting that she has written several scripts and is interested in creative projects, though audience demand often traps her in the "bold" content cycle (14:32-15:43).
She concludes by discussing her daily routine, which she finds quite mundane and boring, and mentions that she is often accompanied to the gym by her boyfriend, who is also her trainer (19:37-21:02).

But more importantly, I've also analyzed hundreds of different channels over the years, and I've discovered something shocking. There's one mistake that can set your channel back by months. This video is specifically for you if you've started a YouTube channel, and you've uploaded one to five videos with disappointing results, and you're tempted to share your channel with friends or family, and you want to build a real business and not just a hobby channel. Here's a mistake that hurts a lot of channels. The moment people share their first video, they immediately share it with friends, family, and their social media followers. They post it on Facebook, send links to their relatives, text their co-workers, and ask everybody they know to subscribe and watch. Now, this seems logical, right? You need views to grow, and you want support from people who care about you. So, it feels natural to reach out to the people closest to you. But here's what most people don't know. This single action can poison your channel's data and confuse YouTube's algorithm for months. Let me show you exactly why this destroys your channel's potential. And trust me, once you understand the math behind this, you will never want to make this mistake. YouTube's algorithm is incredibly sophisticated. We're talking one of the most advanced AI systems in the world. So, when you upload a video, YouTube doesn't just want to show that video to random people. Instead, from my experience, I believe your videos go through what I like to call a four-phase learning process. In the first phase, which happens in the first 24 to 48 hours, YouTube shows your videos to a small test audience. This is based off your thumbnail, title, description, and also your tags. And during this first phase, they're measuring dozens of different data points, including click-through rates, watch time, and engagement patterns. Think of this as YouTube's way of asking, "Is this content any good?" And then in phase two, which happens in week 1 to two, if the initial test goes well, YouTube will start showing your videos to a slightly broader audience, an audience with similar interests, and they're refining their understanding of who is most likely to like your content. This is where YouTube starts to figure out, okay, this content works, but who exactly is it for? Phase three then happens in weeks three to four. And this is where YouTube starts to get really sophisticated. Then they start showing your video to people with very specific viewing patterns and interests. And this is where the magic happens because they're finding your true fans, people who will watch your content religiously and become part of your evergreen audience. And then finally, in phase four, which is an ongoing phase, your video gets recommended on YouTube alongside similar content and shown to people with a proven interest in your niche. This is where YouTube helps your content automatically reach the right audience. And more importantly, this is where you start seeing that beautiful organic growth that every YouTuber dreams of. But here's the most critical part, and honestly, most people completely destroy their chances of YouTube success right here. When you share your video with your personal network, you're essentially breaking this entire learning process. What I mean by this is that when you upload a video, obviously you want that video to get views. You naturally start thinking of sharing your videos with friends and family. But let me tell you something. As soon as you do this, you're going to completely destroy that video's metrics. Let me give you a concrete example of how this works mathematically. Let's say you create a faceless channel about cryptocurrency investing. Your ideal audience is say people from 25 to 45 who are interested in financial education and have disposable income to invest. Now if you share your crypto video with a 100 people from your personal network and this doesn't mean you have to have 100 friends. Let's say you post it on your personal Facebook or your personal Instagram or something like that and you get a 100red views from that and maybe you think some of those views are actually interested in crypto content which a couple might be. Here's what actually happens. about 20 people will actually watch out of politeness, giving you a 20% click-through rate, which actually sounds pretty good, right? However, click-through rate is not the only thing you need. And click-through rate when it's coming from an external source does not matter nearly as much. You see, those 20 friends or family members will probably watch your video for an average of 30 seconds for an 8 to 10-minute video. That's barely even a 10% audience retention rate, which is absolutely terrible. And of course, barely anyone would subscribe because they're probably not even interested in cryptocurrency. And then YouTube sees this data and what is YouTube logically going to think? They're going to say, "Hm, we shared this crypto video with 100 people, but we got a terrible audience retention. Maybe the content's not engaging or maybe we're not showing the content to the right people." But now compare that to when you let YouTube find your viewers organically. If you let YouTube show your videos to a 100 people, which it will, YouTube will test your content to specific audiences if you put the right title, description, and tags on it. And most likely, if you do this the right way, they're going to show your video to people who are actually interested in crypto content. Let's say only eight people click, though, so you only have like an 8% click-through rate. It is a lot lower. However, that's an 8% click-through rate on the actual platform of YouTube. And then let's say those eight people watch for an average of four minutes on an eight-minute video. That's a 50% audience retention, which is amazing. And three of those people actually subscribed because they're genuinely interested in the content. YouTube sees this second scenario and they think, "Wait a second, this video actually is doing good to people who are interested in crypto and finance. Let's show it to more people like that." And the difference is night and day. In the first scenario, you taught YouTube that your content doesn't work. In the second scenario, you let YouTube discover that your content works amazingly well, but only to the right audience. Now, here's where this gets incredibly expensive in terms of your time and potential revenue. Let's look at what happens in the course of several months. In the first few weeks, if you've polluted your content by sharing it with friends and family, YouTube will then keep showing your video to random audiences who aren't interested. Let's say your average retention stays around 15%. You get maybe 500 monthly views and gain five subscribers of which maybe not all of them are even interested in your content. And even after a few months, YouTube is still confused about who the right audience for your content should be. When you pollute that data with viewers who are never going to be interested in your content in the first place, then you're essentially sabotaging their ability to do so. And making these innocent mistakes can mean that all of your hard work, your videos, and your progress gone in a second. If you're serious about growing on YouTube, you need to avoid these. Let's break them down for you right now. Firstly, you may have seen the rise of small creators gathering in the comment sections of my videos and other people's videos too where they basically ask people to sub to their channel. And you might even be one of these yourself. Now, look, I get it. Many of you are looking for a way to get your channel out there and bag a few new subscribers, which is absolutely fine if that's what you want to do. However, if you are getting involved in these, you need to be extremely careful because if you do this wrong, which a lot of you do, you will get your channel banned. Here's an email I got from one of my subscribers a few weeks ago. She was just another regular small channel and she saw that everyone was subscribing to each other in the comment section. So my advice here is if you are going to get involved in these, don't spam the same comment over and over again. You need to be unique and add value to the group or just stay away from it altogether. Now the next common mistake small channels need to avoid making is adding too many hashtags to your videos. And this applies to shorts too. Now you might be thinking, but what's the big deal? Like surely hashtags aren't that dangerous. Well, YouTube literally has this on their website. It says overtagging. If a video or a playlist has more than 60 hashtags, we'll ignore each hashtag on that content. Overtagging may result in the removal of your video from your uploads or from searches. So, they're literally saying if you try and stuff as many hashtags in as possible onto your videos, YouTube will remove them and your account will end up getting shadowbanned because the algorithm would just assume you're a bot account. So, I'd personally recommend just using anywhere from three to six hashtags. I mean, you can use it to 59, but to be honest, I think it's much more worth your time to pick out three to six highly targeted hashtags related directly to your niche. When it comes to tags, there are also some very strict rules you need to follow with these two, but I'll discuss that later on in the video. Now, the next mistake that would get you banned is using other people's content without their permission. Now, I know a lot of you will have seen YouTube experts, including me, telling you to use a tactic called viral replication, where you basically look out into your niche, identify successful videos, and then use them as inspiration for your own. Now, this tactic can work wonders for small channels, but only if you do it properly. And if you do it wrong, it will lead to you receiving copyright strikes or worse, having your account taken off the platform altogether. Now, I've seen many small channels copy viral videos but barely make any changes whatsoever from the original. So, if you're someone who uses a viral replication strategy, you need to make sure that the content itself is actually your own words and your own work. Like, don't just copy the transcript of someone's video and then use it as your own. Like, I promise you, YouTube will ban you for it. And on the topic of copyright, you even need to be careful of images you're using for your thumbnails, too. Like if you're taking images for thumbnails like off the web from Google or somewhere you don't have permission to the owner of that image can write a formal copyright complaint and I have seen small channels being removed simply because of their thumbnails. So always make sure that you have permission to use images in your thumbnails as well if you take them off the web. But now, let's also talk about your overall online safety now. Because as a content creator, you're pretty much putting yourself out there on the entire internet for everyone to see. And that means you'll be interacting with all sorts of people. Some good, some not so good. And if you're not careful, you could leave the door open to some serious problems into your life. And this is where small channels need to be a bit more mindful with what you're doing on the platform. Now, the first thing you need to consider when protecting your actual YouTube account is don't use the same email on your channel description page as the one tied to your actual YouTube account. This is because if someone gets their hands on that email, they could potentially misuse it to cause you trouble with your YouTube account. And it also makes it much easier for people to gain access to your channel if they're determined enough. So, the solution here is to create a completely separate Gmail account that you use for public use. This would be the email that you put on your about me like channel description. It's the one that brands, fans, and potential collaborations will contact you through. This keeps things professional and more importantly keeps your personal YouTube account safe and secure. And I think it would actually genuinely surprise you at just how much of your personal information is floating around online. And I don't just mean your name or your email. I'm talking about phone numbers, location data, and even the websites you visit and how long you spend on them. And the scary part is you probably never gave permission for most of it. And that's because there are hundreds of shady companies out there called data brokers. And their entire business model is collecting your personal data, packaging it up, and selling it to advertisers, scammers, or whoever is willing to pay for it. Now, as a YouTuber, especially if you're just starting out, you're already exposing a lot of yourself just by uploading videos. But once your email is out there too, and once you've signed up for a few tools or made your contact info public, your digital footprint starts growing fast and it becomes harder and harder to stay in control of what's being shared about you. So, the second thing you need to consider when thinking about the safety of your YouTube account is connecting your channel to too many apps. And this is another big mistake I see small channels make, and it's where they're connecting their YouTube channel to way too many third party apps. And I get it. Look, there are so many new AI softwares out there right now. And some of these tools might be things like analytics dashboards, editing software, or even AI tools that help your channel grow faster. But every single app you have connected to your Google account is like opening a tiny door to your channel. And the more doors you have open, the easier it is for someone to sneak in. Now, here's where it gets a little bit risky. Like, even if one of those third party tools gets compromised, or worse, if it's actually just a scam tool designed to look legit, but really just harvest your data, your entire account could be vulnerable. And that means someone else could gain access to your user channel. It could change your login info or even delete everything you've worked so hard to build. So, here's a fix. Go to google.com/acount. Click on security. Scroll down to third party apps with account access and start removing anything you don't absolutely need. Like if it's not essential to your channel or if you don't recognize it, get rid of it. Like on my channels, I only ever have a couple of reputable brands on there that I know I can trust. And you should only be connecting to tools that you 100% trust, too. And this is just another simple way to protect your channel from potential threats. And the final point of the channel safety topic before we talk about how tags can be dangerous. I want to first talk about the most common way I see small creators get their channels hacked. And this is clicking on dodgy links in emails. As you grow on YouTube as a small channel, you'll start getting loads of emails that look like they're from brands wanting to collaborate. But not all of them are real. Some of them are just fishing scams. They'll send you what looks like a normal business email, maybe even with a fake YouTube logo or official sounding name and ask you to do things like review their product or check out that media kit or even something like someone has shared a video link with you. But the moment you click on a dodgy link, you could be taken to things like a fake login page designed to steal your data or you could unknowingly download malware that gives them access to your Google account. And I've seen channels with thousands of subscribers completely wiped out because of one wrong click. So, here's how to stay safe. Never click on links and emails unless you're absolutely sure it's from a trusted source. Like, double check the sender's email address. Look out for weird grammar or formatting. And if something feels off, just trust your gut. And if a brand reaches out and you're unsure, just Google them. Check out if they've worked with other creators or even ask other YouTubers in that space. - How Can Reverse Image Search Protect You Online Dating? - The Love Workshop: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How can reverse image search protect you online dating? Imagine scrolling through a dating profile and seeing a picture that looks too perfect or unfamiliar. You might wonder if it's real or just someone pretending to be someone else. That's where reverse image search comes in. It's a simple tool that helps you verify if the photos on a profile are genuine. You upload or copy the image from the profile and search the internet to see where else that picture appears. If the same photo shows up on multiple profiles, especially on sites that don't match the person's story, it could be a sign of a fake profile or scam. Scammers often steal photos from real people or use stock images to create false identities. Reverse image search helps you spot these tricks early. It can also confirm if the person you are talking to is real, which builds trust and makes your online interaction safer. It's especially useful because romance scams often start with fake images designed to lure you in emotionally or financially. By checking images beforehand, you can avoid falling for these scams and protect your feelings and money. Keep in mind though that some scammers use advanced technology like artificial intelligence to create images that don't exist anywhere else online. These are called deep fake images and they can be harder to detect with just reverse image search. That's why it's smart to combine this tool with other safety habits. Never share too much personal information too soon and watch four signs of inconsistent behavior. Also, using different photos on your own profiles than what you share on other social media accounts can help keep your privacy safe. Overall, reverse image search is a handy digital shield that helps you navigate online dating more securely. It gives you a way to verify images and avoid deception so you can focus on building genuine connections without worry. Remember, staying cautious and using all available tools makes your online dating experience safer and more enjoyable.

- Mastering Reverse Image Search: Find, Verify, and Explore with Google: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] thank you hey everyone welcome back to decryptor today we have got some exciting to learn about reverse image search have you ever wondered where an image came from or if it has been used somewhere else on the internet well today I am going to show you that by just using Google reverse image search feature please don't write in so what is reverse image search all right before we jump into the Practical steps let's understand what reverse image actually is essentially it's a way to It's a tool that lets you upload a picture or paste its link into a search engine to see where else that image appears on the internet. This is especially useful in online dating because scammers and fake profiles often steal photos from other websites or social media accounts. By doing a reverse image search, you can check if the person's profile picture is used elsewhere like onstock photo sites or multiple dating profiles. If the same photo shows up in different places, it's a red flag that the profile might be fake or misleading. Using this tool helps you avoid falling for romance scams where fraudsters use attractive but fake images to gain trust and manipulate emotions. It also helps confirm if the person you're talking to is using their real photo, which builds confidence in your connection. Sometimes scammers use the same photo across many profiles or even create fake social media accounts with the same images. Reverse image search can reveal those patterns and help you spot suspicious activity. Keep in mind that scammers are becoming more clever and may use AI generated images that don't exist anywhere else online. So, while reverse image search is a great safety step, it shouldn't be your only line of defense. Always be cautious with personal details. Watch four red flags like inconsistent stories or reluctance to meet in person and stick to reputable dating platforms. For your own safety, consider using different photos on your social media accounts and dating profiles. That way, you limit what others can find out about you through image searches. Think of reverse image search as a digital detective that helps you verify identities and avoid deception. It's a simple, accessible way to add an extra layer of protection to your online dating journey. Using it regularly can help you spot fake profiles early and make your experience safer and more enjoyable.

Buried deep in settings are a couple of options&nbsp; that, if enabled, automatically&nbsp; send your device data&nbsp; and even your location&nbsp; information straight to Apple.&nbsp; And here's the kicker. Major iOS updates have&nbsp;&nbsp; a habit of flipping these settings right back on without asking you first.&nbsp; iPhone analytics and iCloud analytics let Apple collect data about&nbsp;&nbsp; how you use your phone and the apps on your phone.&nbsp; Apple says it helps them improve&nbsp; their products and services.&nbsp; That can include things&nbsp; like which apps you're using&nbsp; and for how long and where you're using those apps in the physical world.&nbsp; So your iPhone could be logging&nbsp; and secretly sending things&nbsp; back to Apple. So why does it matter?&nbsp; First, it's data leaving your device. Apple says this data is anonymized.&nbsp; It doesn't personally identify you. But if you're someone who values digital privacy,&nbsp; like I do, this feels like one of those just because you can collect it doesn't&nbsp; mean you should situations. Second, and more concerning, iOS updates&nbsp; have been caught turning&nbsp; these settings right back on,&nbsp; even if you turn them off before. Researchers found this happen in iOS 17.&nbsp; And if you haven't double checked your settings since the last update, you could be&nbsp; sharing all this information&nbsp; again without even knowing it.&nbsp; Also, if you've agreed to share analytics and have Location Services turned on,&nbsp; Apple can get location data too. They say it's to improve things like&nbsp;&nbsp; call quality or Apple Pay. It'll turn off Apple Pay.&nbsp; I'll turn off Fitness Plus&nbsp; in Siri, Assistive Voice,&nbsp; and AR Location Accuracy. Just turn them all off&nbsp;&nbsp; if you want maximum privacy. And honestly, if Apple wants feedback,&nbsp;&nbsp; they can ask for it, which they do.&nbsp; You can go to apple.com slash feedback. Apple shows ads in their own apps&nbsp; like the App Store, News and the Stocks app. The personalized ad setting lets Apple&nbsp;&nbsp; use your app activity what you search for,&nbsp;&nbsp; what you read, what you download to target those ads to you.&nbsp; Apple calls this first party data usage. So it's not as creepy as third party tracking.&nbsp; It kind of means that they collected it, so they're using it&nbsp; and they're not selling it to somebody else. But it's still your behavior being&nbsp;&nbsp; used to sell you stuff. And that's the weird part.&nbsp; Apple made a huge deal about&nbsp; app tracking transparency&nbsp; to stop other companies from tracking you. The next really important setting, which is even more dangerous than always, is the precise location switch, which Apple gave us back in September 2020 with iOS 14. Let me show you just how important this switch is using Google Maps. I have two phones here. One with Google Maps with precise location turned on and another with precise location turned off. With precise location on, you see the familiar blue dot. If you zoom way, way, way in, you'll notice that the blue dot actually has a small blue circle around it. This is about as precise as precise location gets. Google Maps knows you're within about a 10-ft circle. Now, I'll open Google Maps on my phone with precise location turned off. We still have a blue dot, but if I zoom way out, we're going to see that the circle is actually pretty huge. And the center of the blue dot is in the wrong place. All Google Maps knows is that I'm somewhere within this kilometer sized circle. It's not very precise. And here's the truth. Companies hide behind the concept of anonymizing your data. When you use your phone, it's not your name or email address associated with the data. It's an anonymous device identifier. So, they're not collecting data about you specifically. They're collecting data about a device that's supposedly not linked to you. However, if they're getting precise location data, it becomes insanely easy to identify you specifically. MIT researchers found that with just four random location points, they could uniquely identify 95% of people from a data set of 1.5 million users. So anonymized data is meaningless if precise location is turned on. If it's off, then you're anonymous within a giant circle. So unless you're living with the bears in northern Alaska, a data broker is going to have a tough time figuring out exactly who you are. Back again to the main page of location services settings. I highly recommend going through and changing every app from always to while using the app or never. This is where Tik Tok got in trouble because in addition to just being an app where you can watch videos, it's a video editing app that people use to, among other things, add filters and censor inappropriate content. Well, until recently, as soon as you started to edit a video on your iPhone, that entire video was instantly pre-uploaded to Tik Tok servers in China. Regardless of whether you chose to save it on your phone or post it on their platform, they help themselves to your videos. None of us know the whole story of the Tik Tok saga, but it stands to reason that people were getting blackmailed, and that's why Tik Tok suddenly became a national security issue. Long story short, be careful about which apps have access to your photos. Let's go back to settings. Scroll down and tap on analytics and improvements. Suffice to say, it's not necessary for you to be sending information about how you use your phone to Apple or third party app developers. This is another setting that is way too vague for me. I turn all these switches off. So, I'll tap the top and just keep turning the switches off. Next, back to the main privacy and security page, settings. And right underneath analytics and improvements, we have Apple advertising. Let's tap to open that up. And we're going to turn off the switch next to personalized ads. This doesn't mean you'll see fewer ads. It means that the ads you'll see will be relevant to the content you're looking at. Meaning, if you're on Disney's website, you'll see ads maybe for other vacation spots, but not for the pair of underwear you looked at 2 weeks ago on some other website. Back to the main page of settings. I'll tap back and let's tap to open up app privacy report and turn this on if it isn't already for you. At some point I might do a video about how to make sense of all this data. And it is safe to have the switch on because this data never leaves your device. And if you want to know when that video comes out, tap the subscribe button and hit that notifications bell. I really appreciate it. Next, let's tap screen and tap to open up Apple Intelligence Report. Same deal as the last setting. When we come back in a future video to dive deep into this, it'll be interesting to have the data to look at and it never leaves your device. Let's tap back to the main page of privacy settings. And down here at the bottom is wired accessories. Tap to open that up. I can't change it on my phone because this phone is running a beta, but on your phone, change it to ask for new accessories. This prevents juicejacking when you plug your iPhone into a public charger that could potentially hack your phone. Next, let's tap back to the main page of privacy settings and tap to open up stolen device protection. With stolen device protection off, if somebody steals your phone and they know your passcode, they could change your Apple ID password and permanently lock you out of your iCloud account and your iPhone. Stolen device protection puts an hour-long delay on making any big changes to your Apple account, which gives you enough time to go to icloud.com/find and lock everything down. You can choose to require security delay when you're away from familiar locations or always. If there are people coming in and out of your home that you don't trust 100%, you might want to set it to always. If you haven't watched my last video about iOS 26, you should check it out. I'm looking forward to making the turn on video that I promised last time around, but unfortunately, I got super sick after I put out the last video, so I wasn't able to record it. My body waited until iOS 26 came out to get bronchitis. I'm feeling a little better now, but you can probably hear it in my voice. I'm not the guy to be alarmist or believe in conspiracies. It's about giving you information so you can decide what to think and who to trust. Hopefully, you trust me. Thanks for watching and I'll see you

- Stop Stress: Tips for Stress Management and Stress Reduction: Kind: captions Language: en there are two types of stress that I'm gonna talk about in this video primary stress and secondary stress primary stress is the stress we experience from the actual stressful events that are going on in our lives being swamped at work we're trying to juggle work-life balance we're having issues in our personal life these can all be really stressful but there are steps we can take to help manage our primary stress because it involves things that are actually happening right now secondary stress is the stress that we add on top of everything else as a result of how we react to our primary stress and in particular the thoughts we have about our stress when we're feeling stressed we tend to worry a lot and we ask ourselves all sorts of what-ifs what if I can't deal with this what if I don't get it done on time what if I do a bad job what if I let people down and once we start going down this path there's no end to the what-ifs and they lead us to all sorts of catastrophic predictions I'm going to lose my job over this and then I won't be able to pay rent or afford my mortgage and I won't be able to support myself or my family and we're all going to end up homeless and living on the streets or I can't deal with this it's too much I'm going to make myself sick with all of this stress and then what am I going to do aren't helpful to us at all they only end up making us more stressed and our stress more difficult to manage our primary stress involves things that are actually going on in our lives right now there are things we can do to address it and the most important thing is to recognize as soon as possible when we're first starting to feel stressed because that's when we have the most chance to be able to do something about and anything we can do to keep our stress at a more manageable level and prevent things from escalating is going to be more effective than trying to deal with our stress once it's already overwhelming and often the first sign that we're starting to feel stressed is something physical our stomach starts churning there's a tightness in our chest or throat in her neck or shoulders or maybe we start to get a headache learning to notice when and where in our body were first starting to feel stressed we have the opportunity to do something about our stress before it's too late and responding to our stress sooner rather than later is probably the single most important thing we can do to make our stress more manageable otherwise we can end up in a straw that broke the camel's back situation where whatever is happening right now may not be too stressful to bear on its own but combined with the accumulation of all of the other stress that's been building up we just can't take it anymore and we get overwhelmed whereas if we found a way to unload some of that straw along the way then this last straw of stress alone wouldn't be enough to break our backs until the first step in managing stress is to notice as soon as you can and acknowledge to yourself that you're starting to feel stressed and the next step is to do something about your stress before it starts to feel out of control often the natural inclination when we're feeling stressed is to try to keep going and going and going until we get everything done but not only does this increase our level of stress exponentially it also makes us far less efficient and we're much better off both in terms of stress and efficiency if we take frequent breaks and make sure we don't work through our lunches and coffee breaks and just give ourselves a chance to unwind a bit and let some of our stress go throughout the day and related to this is to just do one thing at a time when we're feeling stressed it's tempting to on a multitask and try to get everything done at once but again this only increases our level of stress and makes us less efficient and if you have lots of stressful things going on in your life try to only focus on the one that's in front of you right now so if you're stressed at work don't allow your mind to use this as an invitation to start thinking about all the stress you have in other areas of your life because there's nothing we can do about that other stress right now except worry about it and create all sorts of secondary stress for ourselves on top of whatever primary stress were already experiencing and making that primary stress even more difficult to manage to find ways to frame our days with something relaxing so starting off the day with a routine that allows us to ease into our days rather than just going and going and going from the moment we wake up and then at the end of the day finding a way to relax and unwind and framing our days like this with something relaxing increases our resiliency and our ability to deal with stress throughout the day and prevents our stress from carrying over from one day into the next and then when we do start to feel stressed take a moment to ask ourselves whether this is primary stress or secondary stress and if it's primary stress since it's something tangible that's actually going on right now figure out what we can do to start reducing our stress and spend some time problem-solving and strategizing and come up with a plan to help us start tackling whatever it is it's causing a stress and the stopped exercise that I described in another video is a great way to do this but if it's secondary stress that we're experiencing then there's nothing actionable we can do about it because our secondary stress involves worrying about things that haven't happened yet and since they only exist in the future there's nothing we can do about them now except worry about them or learn to let go of them so that we don't add this secondary level of stress on top of whatever primary stress were already experiencing but not worrying can be a lot easier said than done so What if we told you that something as invisible as stress could secretly be aging you faster than time itself? It's a biological mystery. Stress accelerates the aging process in ways that we never see coming. But here's the catch. You can break this cycle. In the next few minutes, we're going to uncover the science behind stress and show you how to take back control, feel younger, and age gracefully. Welcome to Healthy Be, where health and science converge to help you live better. The stress response, an ancient mechanism at play. Imagine being chased by a wild animal. Your body responds in seconds. Your heart races, your muscles tense up, and your mind sharpens. This is the fight orflight response. In the past, it kept us alive in dangerous situations. But today, stress is everywhere, from work pressures to financial struggles, and it never switches off. Our bodies release cortisol, the stress hormone, in response to these pressures. And when it stays elevated over time, it reprograms your biological clock. How stress speeds up aging, the hidden mechanism. When stress becomes chronic, it starts to affect your cells. Let's talk about telomeres, the protective caps at the end of your chromosomes. Every time your cells divide, they shorten a little. But stress, it accelerates this process, causing your telomeres to shorten faster. Shorter telomeres mean faster aging and increased vulnerability to age related diseases. It's like a ticking time bomb inside your DNA. The visible consequences. Stress reveals itself. Stress doesn't just age you internally. You can see it on your face. Ever notice how stress gives you those dark circles under your eyes? That's because of the increased release of cortisol, which affects your skin's elasticity. Then there's wrinkling, premature graying, and hair thinning. Stress also messes with your immune system, leaving you more susceptible to colds and chronic conditions. Real life impact: stress at work, at home, and everywhere. Have you ever wondered why some people seem to age faster than others? It's not just genetics. High stress lifestyles, like those of caregivers, high-powered executives, or healthare workers, contribute to biological aging. The science is clear. Work stress and emotional stress speed up the aging process. And studies show that stressed individuals experience an accelerated decline in their brain function and physical appearance. Reversing stress's effects. It's possible. Now, here's the game changer. You can fight back against stress and its aging effects. It starts with mindfulness. Just a few minutes of deep breathing can lower cortisol levels and restore balance. Next, engage in regular physical activity to trigger the release of endorphins, the body's natural anti-aging hormones. Nutrition is also crucial. Antioxidants from fruits and vegetables can help repair cellular damage. Most importantly, sleep is your secret weapon. During deep sleep, your body repairs and rejuvenates itself. The power of technology and stress management. In today's world, we can use technology to help manage our stress. There are wearables that track your stress levels, apps that guide you through meditation, and AI platforms that offer personalized stress reduction strategies. This modern approach means you have the power to take control. With just a few taps on your phone, you can lower your stress and preserve your youth. Conclusion: The ultimate strategy to reverse stress aging. Here's the truth. Stress will always be a part of life, but its effects don't have to define your health or your age. By understanding the signs of stress and implementing small changes, you can slow down the clock. Start today. Embrace mindfulness, exercise regularly, and nourish your body with the right foods. You are in control of how stress affects you. And with the right mindset and tools, you can reverse its aging effects. You're just one decision away from taking back your youthful energy.

- How to set up an ANONYMOUS iPhone & Apple Computer (2021): Kind: captions Language: en ﻿We carry devices everywhere.&nbsp; They connect us with loved ones,&nbsp;&nbsp; coworkers, and ultimately the entire planet. &nbsp; This new digital landscape is also&nbsp; filled with people trying to monitor&nbsp;&nbsp; these activities and figure out exactly&nbsp; what we’re doing on our devices. Do you use a phone? You have a tracking device? Do&nbsp; you make a call? It was probably intercepted. I think you should assume any communication,&nbsp; that is electronic, is being monitored. Companies create profiles of us and&nbsp; track us across websites for advertising&nbsp;&nbsp; reasons. (server icon with ominous red&nbsp; flashing led light) Internet service&nbsp;&nbsp; providers log our actions. (capitol building&nbsp; icon, and surveillance cameras) Governments&nbsp;&nbsp; dragnet capture everything we do online through&nbsp; programs like TEMPURA, TURMOIL, or they siphon&nbsp;&nbsp; this information from the companies we interact&nbsp; with through programs like PRISM. Via all manner of methods, the activities&nbsp; we engage in in the digital realm are&nbsp;&nbsp; quietly recorded, and neatly stored&nbsp; in giant databases, creating permanent&nbsp;&nbsp; records attached to our identity. They've installed permanent installations&nbsp;&nbsp; to collect people's communications forever. But what if we don’t want our communications&nbsp;&nbsp; collected? what if we don’t want all of our online&nbsp; activity to be viewable by strangers, what can we&nbsp;&nbsp; do? There is no single solution, because “There is actually no agreed definition about&nbsp;&nbsp; privacy.” Privacy is a spectrum, and as&nbsp;&nbsp; celebrated economist Thomas Sowell once said: “There are no solutions; only trade-offs.” The tradeoff for privacy is usually with&nbsp; convenience. Depending on your threat model,&nbsp;&nbsp; some of you might be more willing to&nbsp; sacrifice convenience than others. Some may abstain from digital&nbsp; activities altogether. Most of these people are not&nbsp; ready to be unplugged Then there are privacy solutions that involve&nbsp; knowing how to use command-line code and&nbsp;&nbsp; setting up graphene on an android,&nbsp; and linux on a computer. I’ll go over these setups in other videos.&nbsp; but For THIS privacy quest graphene phone,&nbsp;&nbsp; add a shield about ¾ of the way to the right of&nbsp; the spectrum, and add a mini apple logo and mini&nbsp;&nbsp; guy fawkes mask. Have large images on screen of an&nbsp; iphone and a macbook) I wanted to see if I could&nbsp;&nbsp; set up an anonymous apple computer and iphone&nbsp; that wasn’t in any way tied to my identity. I&nbsp;&nbsp; chose Apple products because they’re mainstream,&nbsp; and the more people exploring privacy options,&nbsp;&nbsp; the better. Once you learn how your&nbsp; identity is tied to your devices,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can make more privacy-conscious choices. So is an anonymous apple device even possible? Let’s find out. First step. Buying your new devices. Your&nbsp; first instinct, like mine, might be to go&nbsp;&nbsp; to the Apple Store online and order what you&nbsp; want. To purchase online, you hand over all&nbsp;&nbsp; manner of sensitive information. Delivery address. Email for receipt.&nbsp;&nbsp; Credit card information. The reason this matters is&nbsp;&nbsp; because you’re not buying generic&nbsp; machines that are interchangeable. Your phone has a unique IMEI, which stands&nbsp; for International Mobile Equipment Identity,&nbsp;&nbsp; and your computer has a unique MAC address which&nbsp; stands for media access control address. These are specific identifiers, and the&nbsp; store knows the exact IMEI or MAC address&nbsp;&nbsp; of the device they’re selling you. So if&nbsp; you’ve provided your credit card number,&nbsp;&nbsp; home address, or email, you’re immediately&nbsp; tied your identity to your new devices. So instead, I went to a store in person,&nbsp; and paid using cash. I’d never noticed how&nbsp;&nbsp; much personal information the store attendees&nbsp; take down just in the process of small talk,&nbsp;&nbsp; until privacy became my concern. It turns out just to purchase&nbsp;&nbsp; a computer or phone can be an invitation&nbsp; to pretty hardcore interrogation. Can I help you with anything? My conversation went something like this: Brand new MacBook, eh? Work or personal use? Neither… uh both And you made an appointment,&nbsp;&nbsp; right, let’s pull up your personal details here? An appointment? What’s your Apple ID? Do I have to submit one- First and last name? I’m not sure that’s necessary- Email? Would prefer not to give it Occupation? Something generic and unsuspicious Let me connect your work to your&nbsp; profile, you might get a group discount That does sound, ooh almost got me Where do you live? Oh wow- Oh, you’re paying with&nbsp;&nbsp; cash? Married? Single? Divorced? YOU’RE&nbsp; BREAKING YOUR MOTHER’S HEART! If you manage to purchase your devices with cash,&nbsp; without handing over any specific information&nbsp;&nbsp; about yourself, you’re doing well! Thanks so much! Email for receipt? Oh, I see what you did&nbsp;&nbsp; there. I’ll just get a printed copy thanks. It was kind of a big deal to opt-out of an emailed&nbsp;&nbsp; receipt. The store assistant literally&nbsp; wrote “opt-out” on their tablet like I&nbsp;&nbsp; was at a TSA screening, and warned me of&nbsp; the dangers of a physical receipt because&nbsp;&nbsp; it could be lost and couldn’t be replaced.&nbsp; They really do try their darndest to tie&nbsp;&nbsp; these devices to you somehow. Again we go back to that spectrum For the more hardcore privacy folk, you might&nbsp; be concerned about the cameras in the parking&nbsp;&nbsp; lot that can see your license plate. The average American is caught on camera&nbsp;&nbsp; 238 times each week. Or you might be concerned that&nbsp;&nbsp; your old phone is in your pocket pinging&nbsp; Apple’s wifi and telling them who has&nbsp;&nbsp; entered. But worrying about these things is&nbsp; way beyond most people’s privacy needs. So now you have your devices. If you purchased a new phone and new&nbsp;&nbsp; computer at the same time, keep in mind their&nbsp; identity can now be tied together. We’re joined? please? Next step, to set up both your phone and&nbsp;&nbsp; computer you require wifi. The moment you take&nbsp; them home and connect them to your home wifi,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ve lost your anonymity. Privacy experts like Michael Bazzel&nbsp;&nbsp; recommend that you set up your devices in store&nbsp; before you leave apple. I actually tried that,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the store assistant said I couldn’t&nbsp; and politely ushered me out. So, I took my devices to a cafe, where I could&nbsp; use their WiFi, in order to try to outsmart the&nbsp;&nbsp; anti-privacy gods. “I am so smart. I&nbsp;&nbsp; am so smart. S. M. R. T. I mean, S. M. A. R. T.” Setting up the computer: You’ll skip the transfer of data option. Disable location services. And you’ll skip the part about&nbsp; setting up an Apple ID: You have the option to set up touch ID&nbsp; or to not. Apple says your fingerprint&nbsp;&nbsp; is encrypted and stored locally on your own&nbsp; device, but if that still makes you uneasy,&nbsp;&nbsp; just skip this step and use your password&nbsp; to unlock the computer instead. Turn on FileVault disk encryption, which makes&nbsp; the content of your device more secure. Then we get to iCloud. iCloud backups are not encrypted. Apple had fully intended to add end to&nbsp; end encryption for iCloud backups, but&nbsp;&nbsp; in 2018 abandoned the plan because&nbsp; they received pushback from the FBI. Not their greatest moment. This means that all the contents of&nbsp;&nbsp; your device are fully accessible, via these&nbsp; icloud backups, including texts from iMessage,&nbsp;&nbsp; WhatsApp and other encrypted services. So make sure the box “Store files from&nbsp;&nbsp; Documents and Desktop in iCloud”&nbsp; is not selected. Next, disable Siri. “Siri records Apple users when they have&nbsp;&nbsp; sex! When they’re talking to their doctors, and&nbsp; when they engage in illegal activity like deals.” According to Apple’s user agreement: “By using Siri or Dictation, you agree&nbsp;&nbsp; and consent to Apple’s and its subsidiaries’ and&nbsp; agents’ transmission, collection, maintenance,&nbsp;&nbsp; processing, and use of this information,&nbsp; including your voice input and User Data.” It’s probably better not to have a device&nbsp; listening to what you say at all times. One thing you might want to consider is Setting&nbsp; up a VPN, which is good for protecting your&nbsp;&nbsp; data over public wifi, but it can also help hide&nbsp; your IP address if you wanted to use it at home&nbsp;&nbsp; by routing your traffic via someone&nbsp; else’s server. But keep in mind,&nbsp;&nbsp; VPNs are not anonymous. The VPN provider sees your&nbsp; IP address and the websites you’re visiting. Depending on your threat model,&nbsp; this may be fine with you. I’ll go over some important tips at&nbsp; the end of the video. But for now,&nbsp;&nbsp; your computer is ready to use! Setting up your phone is a little more involved. First select “set up manually” You can choose to set up Face ID or skip: again&nbsp;&nbsp; apple says face ID data doesn't leave your device&nbsp; and is never backed up to iCloud or anywhere else,&nbsp;&nbsp; but if you’re still uneasy just skip. The biggest difference between setting up&nbsp;&nbsp; this vs your computer is apple ID. While you can&nbsp; bypass this on a computer and still use it fine,&nbsp;&nbsp; on an iphone you need an apple ID to use the&nbsp; app store and download apps for your phone. Without an apple ID, your iphone is&nbsp; basically unusable because there are&nbsp;&nbsp; no alternative stores like Fdroid that allow you&nbsp; to download apps from other sources. Protip: Don’t set up your apple ID when&nbsp; you’re first setting up your device,&nbsp;&nbsp; instead select “do it later”. This is because&nbsp; apple ID requires you to add a payment method when&nbsp;&nbsp; you first set up it. But here’s a neat workaround:&nbsp; if you try to download a free app before setting&nbsp;&nbsp; up your apple ID, you’re able to select “none” as&nbsp; your payment option. If you want to purchase paid&nbsp;&nbsp; apps later on, you can always use an app store&nbsp; gift card that you purchase with cash or crypto.&nbsp;&nbsp; But keep in mind, the more apps you add to&nbsp; your device, the less secure it becomes. You can create a new email address&nbsp; directly on your device Protonmail and tutanota are probably my&nbsp; 2 recommended options, but they’re not&nbsp;&nbsp; perfect. Protonmail will require you to verify&nbsp; using a phone number, and tutanota won’t let&nbsp;&nbsp; you create a new account using a VPN, so&nbsp; pick your privacy-undoing poison. Choose, pick your poison just keep in mind that sending emails&nbsp;&nbsp; to and from your existing addresses would&nbsp; link this new address to you. You also have to provide a phone number. Ergh.&nbsp; Unfortunately there’s no way around that.&nbsp;&nbsp; I tried half a dozen different burner number&nbsp; websites, but got an error each time saying&nbsp;&nbsp; “number used too many times”. So I bought a new&nbsp; prepaid SIM using cash, and activated the SIM&nbsp;&nbsp; on the new phone itself over the cafe wifi.&nbsp; The problem with using cash for prepaid SIMs&nbsp;&nbsp; is you can’t just automatically renew it, add&nbsp; credits, or top-up with ease. These are the&nbsp;&nbsp; trade-offs with convenience that privacy advocates&nbsp; have to balance again and again. But your new SIM&nbsp;&nbsp; should work fine for your Apple ID. You will also be required to add a billing&nbsp;&nbsp; address regardless of whether you only choose&nbsp; to download free apps. But it turns out that&nbsp;&nbsp; writing “N/A” or gobeldy gook in every field&nbsp; works fine. You just need a zip code that&nbsp;&nbsp; actually exists, and you’re golden. And voila, 2 new devices that are not&nbsp;&nbsp; attached to your identity! Some other things to remember: If you log into an existing account on your new&nbsp; devices, you’ve undone your privacy. If you connect to your home wifi&nbsp; and visit websites in the clear,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ve undone your privacy. And you may not realize but previous&nbsp;&nbsp; wifi connections can become a fingerprint for your&nbsp; device unless you deselect “remember this network”&nbsp;&nbsp; and “connect automatically” for each one. Ever wondered how your phone knows to&nbsp;&nbsp; automatically connect to your home wifi when&nbsp; you get home? It’s because it’s constantly&nbsp;&nbsp; trying to ping that home wifi whenever your wifi&nbsp; is turned on, no matter where you are. Now think of all the networks your phone connects&nbsp; to automatically, your work, your friends house,&nbsp;&nbsp; that conference you went to once: your phone travels around constantly&nbsp;&nbsp; pinging out that entire list until it&nbsp; eventually gets in range of one of them.&nbsp;&nbsp; That’s a unique identifier for your phone. Forget all networks after using them. You should also turn your devices&nbsp; off when you leave the café, As hardware security expert Bunnie&nbsp; Huang once explained to me: Inside of an iPhone, you have several radios, you&nbsp; have wifi and GPS and cellular and Bluetooth and&nbsp;&nbsp; all these different things. Your phone constantly beaming out&nbsp;&nbsp; all kinds of radio signals can be an&nbsp; effective way to identify you. And finally, try to only have one device&nbsp; turned on at a time. Let’s say that every&nbsp;&nbsp; time your new phone is turned on, it detects&nbsp; your old phone nearby which is also turned on,&nbsp;&nbsp; this can be a way to tie that new device to your&nbsp; existing one. The same goes if you’re traveling in&nbsp;&nbsp; a car and have both devices turned on. If they’re&nbsp; traveling to all the same places at the same time,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s a way to tie their identities together.&nbsp; So Keep your new devices off when you’re home,&nbsp;&nbsp; and just use one device at a time. And if you’re super concerned, you can&nbsp;&nbsp; even get a faraday bag for when your&nbsp; device is not being used. Sometimes your phone can look like it’s off,&nbsp; but it’s actually just pretending to be off and&nbsp;&nbsp; shouting “I am here” Faraday pouch … you can drop&nbsp;&nbsp; off the digital map. Faraday bags can help block&nbsp;&nbsp; these potential signals, and help stop devices&nbsp; being linked to one another. But again, it really&nbsp;&nbsp; depends on how much privacy you’re looking for.&nbsp; It turns out that if you want to set up a truly&nbsp;&nbsp; anonymous digital device, there are all kinds&nbsp; of little things like this to be aware of. And&nbsp;&nbsp; even after your devices are set up, you have to&nbsp; stay ever vigilant because one careless mistake&nbsp;&nbsp; can undermine all your hard-earned privacy. But the good news is that most of us&nbsp;&nbsp; are not Snowden or being&nbsp; targeted by nation states,&nbsp;&nbsp; and don’t need bulletproof privacy. Making just&nbsp; small steps to tidy up our digital footprints&nbsp;&nbsp; actually goes a really long way. Being&nbsp; mindful of how we pay for things,&nbsp;&nbsp; knowing when our devices are pinging out radio&nbsp; signals and perhaps turning them off when we can,&nbsp;&nbsp; understanding that the apps and websites we use&nbsp; are creating profiles of us, so siloing certain&nbsp;&nbsp; apps or activities on separate devices. And at least half the privacy battle is simply&nbsp;&nbsp; understanding how your information is&nbsp; being leaked. But WITH this knowledge&nbsp;&nbsp; you too can start to make small changes to&nbsp; live a more privacy-conscious lifestyle.

- iPhone Security Features You Should Enable Now!: Kind: captions Language: en-US Even if you've been using an iPhone for years,&nbsp; there are some features relating to security that&nbsp;&nbsp; you should probably enable or at least consider&nbsp; enabling depending on your preference and&nbsp;&nbsp; situation. So that is what I'm going to go over.&nbsp; I'm ThioJoe, and let's get into it. I've got about&nbsp;&nbsp; 10 of them. Starting off, we have the accessory&nbsp; trust feature, and this is actually new in iOS 26.&nbsp;&nbsp; And you can find this under settings, privacy and&nbsp; security, then scroll down to the bottom and look&nbsp;&nbsp; for "Wired Accessories." This setting determines&nbsp; whether or not your phone will explicitly ask you&nbsp;&nbsp; if you want to trust accessories connected&nbsp; via USB to a computer or something else. I&nbsp;&nbsp; believe it defaults to "Automatically Allow When&nbsp; Unlocked", but I would personally suggest setting&nbsp;&nbsp; it to "Ask For New Accessories". The reason is&nbsp; that there is potentially a risk for plugging&nbsp;&nbsp; in a USB device and then letting your iPhone&nbsp; trust it. For example, there's been warnings&nbsp;&nbsp; about so-called "juice jacking", where you&nbsp; plug into what you think is a power port,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it ends up being a malicious device. And you&nbsp; could debate whether or not that's actually likely&nbsp;&nbsp; to be a reality. However, let's say it is, and you&nbsp; plug in your phone while you're using it, because&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe it's running low on battery, then if your&nbsp; phone is already unlocked, it's immediately going&nbsp;&nbsp; to have access to your phone. Whereas if you plug&nbsp; it in and it asks whether or not you want to trust&nbsp;&nbsp; some accessory, but you thought it was just a&nbsp; power port, well that would probably set off some&nbsp;&nbsp; alarm bells, and you can avoid that. Though I did&nbsp; notice this might not show up on all models, like&nbsp;&nbsp; my iPhone SE, it didn't show it. Alright next up&nbsp; is a Find My setting called "Send Last Location".&nbsp;&nbsp; You can find this under settings, then click your&nbsp; account at the top, then Find My, Find My iPhone,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then enable the setting called "Send Last&nbsp; Location". This makes it so if your phone's&nbsp;&nbsp; battery is critically low, it will actually&nbsp; send off one last hurrah, basically, so that if&nbsp;&nbsp; you go to check its location, if you lost it or&nbsp; something, it'll have it. This is probably more&nbsp;&nbsp; useful if you legitimately can't remember where&nbsp; your phone went, as opposed to it being stolen.&nbsp;&nbsp; Anyway, apparently for privacy reasons, Apple&nbsp; does not actually check and store the location&nbsp;&nbsp; of your phone constantly. It's only if you do&nbsp; a Find My check on the location of the phone,&nbsp;&nbsp; normally at least. But that also means that if you&nbsp; lost your phone and it died before you realized,&nbsp;&nbsp; then if you went to try and do a Find My check&nbsp; on it, apparently it's just not going to return&nbsp;&nbsp; a location. That is, unless you have this setting&nbsp; enabled, and then it would have done that. So if&nbsp;&nbsp; you legitimately lost your phone, it's probably&nbsp; not going to be moving. So that would be a lot&nbsp;&nbsp; more helpful. Now before I continue, something&nbsp; else that's helpful is from today's sponsor,&nbsp;&nbsp; Ugreen.
