And with all that being said, let's&nbsp; continue. Okay, moving on, we actually have some&nbsp;&nbsp; stuff that you probably want to disable, and&nbsp; it has to do with what stuff is accessible from&nbsp;&nbsp; the lock screen. You can get to these options by&nbsp; going to the settings again, Face ID and Passcode,&nbsp;&nbsp; or Touch ID and Passcode. Then scroll down to the&nbsp; section called Allow Access When Locked. And if&nbsp;&nbsp; I were you, I would probably just disable all&nbsp; the ones that I have disabled here. For Control&nbsp;&nbsp; Center, the reason is that a thief, if they steal&nbsp; your phone, then that means even if the phone is&nbsp;&nbsp; locked, they can swipe down and then toggle off&nbsp; Bluetooth and Wi-Fi and put it into airplane mode&nbsp;&nbsp; and all that, and potentially hinder your ability&nbsp; to actually use the Find My Network. For Siri,&nbsp;&nbsp; I would turn that off because Siri can do a lot.&nbsp; I'd also disable Reply with Message and Return&nbsp;&nbsp; Missed Calls, even if just to prevent accidental&nbsp; texts and phone calls to people. If they called&nbsp;&nbsp; you and for some reason the screen turned on, you&nbsp; don't want to be accidentally dialing people. In&nbsp;&nbsp; the wallet setting, disabling this makes it so&nbsp; even if you double tap the side button while the&nbsp;&nbsp; phone is locked, it will not actually show any&nbsp; info about your cards and stuff. It just makes&nbsp;&nbsp; you do a Face ID authentication. And this one is&nbsp; a no-brainer to me because even if you have it&nbsp;&nbsp; enabled, you can't actually use it to pay unless&nbsp; you do Face ID anyway. So having it on basically&nbsp;&nbsp; just lets anyone double tap and see what kind of&nbsp; cards you have saved in your phone without giving&nbsp;&nbsp; you any extra convenience. It makes no sense.&nbsp; Just turn that off. Home Control, this is another&nbsp;&nbsp; one I think is a no-brainer. I don't think you&nbsp; really want people to, for example, unlock your&nbsp;&nbsp; front door if you have a smart lock or mess with&nbsp; your devices in your physical home with the phone&nbsp;&nbsp; being locked. And even if you don't have any smart&nbsp; home devices you set to control with your phone,&nbsp;&nbsp; you may as well just turn it off just in case for&nbsp; the future. For Today View and Search, this lets&nbsp;&nbsp; you search through and see what apps are on your&nbsp; phone and other stuff. You might not want that.&nbsp;&nbsp; But more importantly, it allows anyone to just&nbsp; swipe left from the screen and see any widgets&nbsp;&nbsp; you have set up there and use them. Or even just&nbsp; accidentally, you're more prone to have it turn on&nbsp;&nbsp; and swipe over and activate one of those things.&nbsp; Better to just have it off. Alright moving on,&nbsp;&nbsp; we have a specific option in the Stolen Device&nbsp; Protection feature. You can find the Stolen Device&nbsp;&nbsp; Protection feature under Privacy and Security.&nbsp; And if you don't have it enabled in general,&nbsp;&nbsp; I would definitely enable the feature. I believe&nbsp; it asks you at one point whether or not you wanted&nbsp;&nbsp; to enable it. So if you didn't, definitely enable&nbsp; it. Anyway, under Security Delay, usually the&nbsp;&nbsp; default option is when you're away from "familiar&nbsp; locations", but I would set this to always. Now&nbsp;&nbsp; as for why this is the case, it takes a bit of&nbsp; context for how this feature works. Basically,&nbsp;&nbsp; how Stolen Device Protection works is it&nbsp; requires you to actually use biometrics,&nbsp;&nbsp; not your phone PIN, for certain critical settings&nbsp; that you might go to change. Or more importantly,&nbsp;&nbsp; if a thief goes to try and change after they&nbsp; watched you put in your PIN or tricked you&nbsp;&nbsp; into doing it, like asking you to use your phone&nbsp; and then make it force lock and then say, "Oh,&nbsp;&nbsp; your phone's locked." And when you go to try and&nbsp; help out and unlock your phone, they watch you and&nbsp;&nbsp; your PIN. And before this feature, simply knowing&nbsp; your phone PIN allowed them to do everything with&nbsp;&nbsp; your whole Apple account, including changing the&nbsp; account password, making it so you were literally&nbsp;&nbsp; never able to recover it again, like setting up&nbsp; a security key, encrypting everything. I mean,&nbsp;&nbsp; it was absolutely insane. So this feature is&nbsp; really important for that by requiring certain&nbsp;&nbsp; things to use biometrics, which obviously a&nbsp; thief is not going to be able to probably do.&nbsp;&nbsp; And then for certain especially important things,&nbsp; like maybe changing your whole account password,&nbsp;&nbsp; there's an additional delay where you have to&nbsp; authenticate with your biometric, like Face ID,&nbsp;&nbsp; then do an hour delay and then authenticate again.&nbsp; And I guess the reason is if a thief steals your&nbsp;&nbsp; phone and they know about this and there was no&nbsp; delay, they could simply go to the setting they&nbsp;&nbsp; want to change, like the most important one, like&nbsp; the Apple account password, and then simply hold&nbsp;&nbsp; it up to your face before you realize what's going&nbsp; on. And then they'd be able to change it and run&nbsp;&nbsp; away or whatever. Whereas with this, they would&nbsp; have to hold it up to your face, wait an hour,&nbsp;&nbsp; find you again and do it again. Unlikely. Anyway,&nbsp; going back to the familiar locations thing,&nbsp;&nbsp; your reason you want to set it to always is&nbsp; Apple does not actually tell you what these&nbsp;&nbsp; familiar locations are or let you set them. There&nbsp; is a thing hidden deep in the settings for common&nbsp;&nbsp; locations you've been to, but there's no way&nbsp; to know when one of those locations becomes&nbsp;&nbsp; a "familiar location". And I would think&nbsp; that if you have a favorite hangout spot,&nbsp;&nbsp; like a cafe or something that you're at a lot,&nbsp; and if it marks it as a familiar location, well,&nbsp;&nbsp; just the fact that you're there more often&nbsp; probably means you're more likely to have&nbsp;&nbsp; your phone stolen there. And then if a thief does&nbsp; that, they could just bypass all your stuff. So I&nbsp;&nbsp; would just set it to always and not worry about&nbsp; it. Okay now moving on, this is more of just a&nbsp;&nbsp; tip if you do happen to lose your phone or have&nbsp; it stolen ever. You probably already know about&nbsp;&nbsp; the Lost Mode that lets you kind of lock down your&nbsp; phone if it's lost and it requires extra stuff to&nbsp;&nbsp; unlock. So a thief can't really use it or try your&nbsp; password, stuff like that. But with that feature,&nbsp;&nbsp; there's an option that lets you display a message&nbsp; on the phone that it's in lost mode and how to&nbsp;&nbsp; contact you. And also separately a phone number to&nbsp; contact you, which will be displayed on the phone.&nbsp;&nbsp; You can see that it says it will be shown. And&nbsp; the tip is I would NOT put your phone number in&nbsp;&nbsp; that spot where it has that. Instead, I would put&nbsp; a new email address in the regular message box.&nbsp;&nbsp; And that's because if it was a thief, thieves are&nbsp; known to try and trick people and threaten them&nbsp;&nbsp; into disabling iCloud lock on the phone so that&nbsp; they can unlock it and then sell it for a bigger&nbsp;&nbsp; price instead of having to part it out if it was&nbsp; locked. So what I would do is instead create a&nbsp;&nbsp; brand new Gmail account or whatever, specifically&nbsp; for someone to contact you if they find your&nbsp;&nbsp; phone. That way it's not any consequential if&nbsp; it was a thief who actually stole it. Whereas&nbsp;&nbsp; otherwise, if you put your actual number, I mean,&nbsp; there's messages of people getting death threats&nbsp;&nbsp; and things like that all from the thief and really&nbsp; nasty messages. And you just don't want them to&nbsp;&nbsp; have your actual contact information. Alright next&nbsp; up, this one is one you might've heard of before,&nbsp;&nbsp; but never really considered that. And that is to&nbsp; use an alphanumeric passcode, not just a bunch&nbsp;&nbsp; of numbers. I personally have a really long&nbsp; alphanumeric passcode. It's not actually as&nbsp;&nbsp; inconvenient as you might think. You almost never&nbsp; have to actually type in your passcode. It really&nbsp;&nbsp; only comes into play for certain specific&nbsp; menus. And if you don't use it very often,&nbsp;&nbsp; I think like once a week, it makes you type it&nbsp; in if you've just been using Face ID all week,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is usually the case. And also if you fail&nbsp; using Face ID too many times, then it'll also&nbsp;&nbsp; make you type it in. But again, for the most&nbsp; part, you don't really need to type it in. So&nbsp;&nbsp; it's not really that inconvenient and it makes&nbsp; it a lot more secure if someone's either trying&nbsp;&nbsp; to watch you type in your passcode. If they see&nbsp; a whole keyboard come up and a bunch of things,&nbsp;&nbsp; they probably would not even bother to try and&nbsp; steal your phone. Or even if it's just one of&nbsp;&nbsp; your friends who wants to prank you and get into&nbsp; your phone, then again, it just makes it harder in&nbsp;&nbsp; general for them to watch you put it in. Alright&nbsp; next up, this is another pretty simple one, and&nbsp;&nbsp; that is to reduce the auto lock display time. You&nbsp; can find this under Display and Brightness, and&nbsp;&nbsp; then Auto Lock. And this just determines how long&nbsp; it takes for your phone to turn off the display&nbsp;&nbsp; and lock if you're not using it. The lower the&nbsp; setting, the more secure your phone. I personally&nbsp;&nbsp; have it set to one minute. And you might think "Oh&nbsp; well that's not long enough. What if I'm watching&nbsp;&nbsp; a video or something and not touching the phone?"&nbsp; Well actually there's another feature in Face ID&nbsp;&nbsp; and passcode called Attention Aware Features and&nbsp; that makes it so as long as you're looking at the&nbsp;&nbsp; phone, then it's not going to lock no matter how&nbsp; long you leave it without touching it. As long as&nbsp;&nbsp; you're looking at the phone, it keeps it unlocked.&nbsp; So the timer really only applies if the phone is&nbsp;&nbsp; set down or something like that. If you do set it&nbsp; to one minute, I found that it will dim the screen&nbsp;&nbsp; about 20 seconds before it turns off or after 40&nbsp; seconds. And if you have it set to 30 seconds,&nbsp;&nbsp; it dims about 10 seconds before turning it off.&nbsp; So you'll at least have kind of a warning. If&nbsp;&nbsp; you don't want it to lock, you can just kind of&nbsp; tap it. Another point though, even if you do want&nbsp;&nbsp; it longer, I would not ever set it to "Never"&nbsp; unless you have a very specific reason. Like&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe the device is meant to be used as a kiosk&nbsp; or something, and you actually want it to be on&nbsp;&nbsp; all day. So if you have it set to never, at least&nbsp; set it to five minutes, but really ideally set it&nbsp;&nbsp; to a one minute or 30 seconds. Next, I figure&nbsp; I may as well at least mention Lockdown Mode,&nbsp;&nbsp; though most people probably shouldn't enable&nbsp; this. This one's under Privacy and Security&nbsp;&nbsp; and then Lockdown Mode. This one does a whole&nbsp; bunch of stuff that is probably overkill and&nbsp;&nbsp; it turns off a lot of features. Like in iMessage,&nbsp; you can't see a lot of image previews for certain&nbsp;&nbsp; types of files and links. I'll probably make&nbsp; a whole separate video about lockdown mode for&nbsp;&nbsp; those who are interested, but the short version&nbsp; is if you don't know what lockdown mode is,&nbsp;&nbsp; you probably don't need it. Alright moving on,&nbsp; we have the feature Require Attention for Face&nbsp;&nbsp; ID. Now I believe this one actually probably is&nbsp; enabled by default, but I think it's actually&nbsp;&nbsp; good to at least know how it works too. The idea&nbsp; behind this feature is it makes it so someone&nbsp;&nbsp; can't just sneakily hold up your phone maybe at&nbsp; an angle where you don't notice and you're not&nbsp;&nbsp; looking at the phone at all. You're looking at&nbsp; something else and someone sneakily gets you to&nbsp;&nbsp; unlock your phone unbeknownst to you. And that's&nbsp; because like the thing suggests, it requires that&nbsp;&nbsp; you actually look at the phone while it checks&nbsp; for face ID. And also a good thing to know is&nbsp;&nbsp; you have to have at least one eye open for it to&nbsp; work. So if you're sleeping, your eyes are closed,&nbsp;&nbsp; it will not unlock for you, even if it's held&nbsp; directly in front of your face. So that's a good&nbsp;&nbsp; thing to know if someone is trying to force you to&nbsp; unlock your phone with face. ID, like holding it&nbsp;&nbsp; up to your face, just close your eyes and then&nbsp; they won't be able to do it. Alright next up,&nbsp;&nbsp; we have Advanced Data Protection. And this is&nbsp; one to maybe consider, but it's not probably&nbsp;&nbsp; for everyone, so you want to think about it. And&nbsp; you could find this under Settings and then your&nbsp;&nbsp; account at the top, iCloud, and then Advanced Data&nbsp; Protection. This basically adds extra encryption&nbsp;&nbsp; and protections to your Apple account. And it&nbsp; basically disables Apple's usual standard account&nbsp;&nbsp; recovery processes, as well as adding extra&nbsp; encryption and stuff. The idea being that no&nbsp;&nbsp; one can just contact Apple pretending to be you,&nbsp; and if they have enough information or something,&nbsp;&nbsp; trick them into giving them access to your&nbsp; account. However, if you choose to enable&nbsp;&nbsp; Advanced Data Protection, you very much need to be&nbsp; careful and ensure that you properly set up enough&nbsp;&nbsp; recovery options. If you do lose access to your&nbsp; account with it enabled, one way you could recover&nbsp;&nbsp; it is with another Apple device. You'd be able&nbsp; to verify on the other one that you're trying to&nbsp;&nbsp; recover it. But if you only have one Apple device,&nbsp; then that's something to consider. That might be&nbsp;&nbsp; risky. However, there are two other options, such&nbsp; as a account recovery key, which is just a big&nbsp;&nbsp; long string of letters and numbers that you could&nbsp; use to recover your account. But you would want to&nbsp;&nbsp; absolutely make sure that you never, ever, ever&nbsp; lose it. You would store it in a secure place,&nbsp;&nbsp; multiple places, safe deposit box, stuff&nbsp; like that. And you also have an option to&nbsp;&nbsp; add a trusted contact so that if you can't access&nbsp; it, then you could allow someone else to maybe&nbsp;&nbsp; generate a recovery key that would apparently&nbsp; work the same way. And also, I would be careful,&nbsp;&nbsp; even if you don't enable Advanced Data Protection,&nbsp; apparently just simply generating a recovery key&nbsp;&nbsp; will turn off the normal account recovery process.&nbsp; So be careful of even doing that, know what&nbsp;&nbsp; you're getting into. So just realize that if you&nbsp; enable Advanced Data Protection or you generate&nbsp;&nbsp; a recovery key and you lose it and you lose your&nbsp; other Apple devices or simply lose access to them,&nbsp;&nbsp; like you forget their passcode or something, you&nbsp; will never be able to access your Apple account&nbsp;&nbsp; again. Even Apple will not be able to recover&nbsp; the data within it. It'll be gone forever. So you&nbsp;&nbsp; might be thinking, "Well this sounds like a scary&nbsp; feature. Why would I ever want to enable that?"&nbsp;&nbsp; But if you're paranoid like me, it probably is&nbsp; worth enabling because it just makes it impossible&nbsp;&nbsp; basically for a bad person to be able to access&nbsp; my account and I'll just be sure to never lose&nbsp;&nbsp; the recovery options. So if you don't really trust&nbsp; yourself that much, then don't enable it. And next&nbsp;&nbsp; we have a feature that is relatively newer and it&nbsp; is the ability to set apps to require Face ID to&nbsp;&nbsp; use them or Touch ID if your phone has that. Now&nbsp; for the longest time, apps could already build&nbsp;&nbsp; this into their app, but this lets you actually do&nbsp; it for any app. You just long press on the app on&nbsp;&nbsp; the home screen and select to require Face ID. So&nbsp; first what I would do is go to any financial apps,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially, and those probably have a built-in&nbsp; feature that requires Face ID. You probably&nbsp;&nbsp; don't have to do it manually, but make sure that&nbsp; it's enabled. If you can't find the feature in&nbsp;&nbsp; the actual app, then you could do it manually.&nbsp; But basically, again, it just goes back to if&nbsp;&nbsp; someone asks to borrow your phone, there's a lot&nbsp; of thieves who are very practiced in very quickly&nbsp;&nbsp; searching and finding certain common financial&nbsp; apps and immediately sending themselves money&nbsp;&nbsp; in a matter of seconds before you even realize&nbsp; what's going on. Whereas if you require Face ID to&nbsp;&nbsp; access it, then they're not going to be able to do&nbsp; that. So just double check anything where money's&nbsp;&nbsp; involved that it requires biometrics. So I would&nbsp; be curious to know which of these you guys already&nbsp;&nbsp; had set up or maybe ones you didn't know about.&nbsp; We could talk about that down in the comments.&nbsp;&nbsp; Thanks again to Ugreen for sponsoring and be sure&nbsp; to check out the Ugreen NASync DH2300 through the&nbsp;&nbsp; link in the description and use the code for 20%&nbsp; off. If you want to keep watching, next up here's&nbsp;&nbsp; a video where I talk about the hidden new features&nbsp; in the latest version of iOS. So a lot of stuff&nbsp;&nbsp; that no one really talks about and Apple didn't&nbsp; even mention anywhere. So I'll put that link&nbsp;&nbsp; right there you can click on. So thanks so much&nbsp; for watching and I'll see you in the next one.

In social media size, we've seen Tik Tok have a massive run and then it dies off at one point. 16:40 also, as a licensed vet tech and rescue worker, I just wanna say that we don’t use dawn dish soap regularly on animals……more and more research and evidence is coming out proving it isn’t good for animals (it should only be used briefly in certain situations, we use it in rescue to start getting rid of large flea burdens, it’s not something you should bathe your pet in regularly, gentle pet safe shampoo and preventatives is what you want)

That's the location of a Gulf cartel safe house in Brownsville. The Sakario thinks he's getting a religious piece to honor his patron saint. What he's actually getting is a permanent map to cartel infrastructure that will be photographed by border patrol when they arrest him in 3 months, analyzed by an FBI tattoo expert, and used to coordinate a raid that captures 15 cartel members. You've tattooed 478 cartel members over the past 4 years. Every single tattoo contains hidden information, coordinates, phone numbers, names, dates, kill counts. Your art is walking evidence. And you're about to learn that when you turn 500 human bodies into permanent criminal records, the FBI doesn't need to prove conspiracy. They just need to decode your work. Your name is David Reyes. 35 tattoo artist in Macallen, Texas, right on the Mexican border. You've been tattooing professionally since 21. You're good. Really good. You specialize in religious imagery, portraits, realistic designs. Your Instagram has 45,000 followers. People drive from Houston, San Antonio, Dallas to get tattooed by you. Clean shop, licensed, respected. McAllen is a border town. Population 150,000, mostly Latino, heavily Catholic, workingass. Also a major corridor for drug trafficking. Gulf cartel controls this region. You see cartel members around town. Everyone does. They're your neighbors, your customers. You tattoo all kinds of people. Construction workers getting memorial pieces, young women getting flowers, guys getting their kids' names, and yes, cartel members. They want religious imagery. Virgin Mary, Christ, St. Michael, crosses. You don't judge. A tattoo artist doesn't get to pick clients based on profession. Your shop is Sacred Ink. Small place, two stations, clean, professional. Waiting list six months. You charge $150 an hour. You make $80,000 yearly. You support your family, wife, two young kids. You own your house. Living a good, honest life doing work you love. Your introduction to corruption. Man named Miguel 3 years ago. Wanted St. Michael tattoo on his back. Full peace. Elaborate. You quoted $3,000 for 20 hours. He agreed immediately. Cash. Over those 20 hours, Miguel talked. professional, polite, educated, explained he worked in import export, appreciated your talent, said his associates might want work. Asked if you do private appointments after hours for special clients who value discretion. He'd pay double. You said yes. Smart business. Private appointments, cash payments, wealthy clients. Not obviously, not like gang tattoos police recognize. They needed something subtle, artistic, something that could identify members to each other without alerting authorities. He asked if you could create a symbol, something religious but with specific details only organization members would recognize a particular style of St. Michael. Specific design of Virgin Mary details in shading, positioning, elements. Could you do that? You said yes. It's just design work. Creating custom symbols is your job. You designed three St. Michael variations for Miguel. Each with slightly different cloud patterns, wing positions, demon designs. He chose one, said it would be their St. Michael. This was marking system. But you rationalized, I'm just doing art. I'm not involved in their business. If they want to use my designs to identify each other, that's their choice. You asked why, Miguel explained. Our members get arrested, phones seized, contacts compromised. But nobody looks at tattoos as evidence. A St. Michael tattoo is just religious art. But if that tattoo contains coordinates to safe houses, phone numbers for contacts, all hidden in design, then every member is a walking encrypted database. That's evidence tampering. That's way beyond just tattoos." You became tattoo artist who hides criminal evidence and artwork. You just committed your first felony. Over next year, you tattooed 127 cartel members. Each tattoo contained hidden information. GPS coordinates and cloud patterns, safe houses, stash locations, drop points, phone numbers and halos, wing feathers, demon scales, contact numbers for higherups, dates and background details, operation dates, shipment schedules, names and decorative scroll work, aliases, code names, numbers in various elements, kill counts, rank indicators, territory markers. You tried to ignore it. I'm just following instructions. Don't know what these coordinates mean, but you knew. Every coordinate you tattooed was crime scene waiting to happen. Every phone number was evidence. You were creating living walking criminal database. Then you made mistake. Regular client, construction worker named Robert, not affiliated with any cartel. Came in wanting St. Michael tattoo. Showed you reference image. It was one of your designs. Seen it on friend's Instagram. Loved it. wanted exact same one. You should have said no. - 6 Signs You Are Being Catfished on Social Media & Dating Sites: Kind: captions Language: en-US Have you met someone online and you're not sure if they are who they say they are? By the end of this video, you will know the key signs that you are being catfished online and what to do if you fall victim to it. Hi. I'm Dayra Schmidt. The term catfishing refers to when a person takes information and images, typically from other people, and uses them to create a new virtual identity for themselves and lure unsuspecting individuals into having a romantic relationship with them. It is very common among dating websites and social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Ashley Madison, and affects millions of people each year. Catfishing is typically the precursor to further unlawful actions such as sextortion, online extortion, and revenge porn. In this video, I'm going to explain the top six signs to look for if you think you're being catfished, how to avoid becoming a victim in these situations, and what to do if you have unfortunately found yourself the victim of catfishing. Pun Intended one of the biggest red flags when talking to people online is how the relationship is initiated. Receiving a friend request online from someone you've never met with no mutual friends or followers should give anyone pause. Take a look at their profile to check if it was made recently or if they have very few friends, followers, or content on their page. Newer profiles or profiles with little interaction with their "friends" usually indicates they are catfishers, trolls or burner accounts. Pay close attention to the photos they have posted. Do they only have professional or model like photos posted? If they only have bikini or extremely filtered photos and not a single photo of them enjoying the simple things in life like a slice of pizza, then your spidey sense should be tingling. Another very overlooked clue of a catfish is having watermarked photos from professional photographers or other dating sites on their profile. Sometimes it's much easier to spot a catfishing attempt because the facts just simply don't add up. Be cognizant of the language being used in their messages for broken English, many one sentence messages in a row instead of paragraphs, and odd use of capitalization and punctuation like overuse of exclamation marks or writing in all caps. Not to say that English is everybody's first language, but if someone is posing as a former cheerleader who has lived in Omaha, Nebraska, their whole life, then they should have a proper grasp on their native language. Make sure the setting matches their location. For example, if someone says they live in Alaska and all of their pictures are of beaches and palm trees, then they may not be telling you the whole story. Lastly, keep in mind what specific times they're communicating with you. Are they messaging you at the time when people would likely be in a deep sleep? Many of the catfishing schemes they have encountered have come from very different time zones, like the Philippines, Ivory Coast, or Morocco. Impersonating a celebrity or socialite who is looking to settle down or posing as a military member with a secret job are two extremely common scenarios where one might look back and say, what was I thinking? Women especially fall for the scams where they fall in love with an attractive military man who just so happens to have a top secret job overseas, and that is why they can't talk to them on the phone or video chat. Scammers in the sextortion category of catfishing treat their schemes like a career. Their job is to quickly gain your trust and your intimate images in a very short period of time, so their messages to you will often quickly turn sexual after a quick introduction. Some good old fashioned Internet fun is nothing to be ashamed of, but if a mysterious profile is asking you your name, location, marital status, occupation, and pictures of your naked body in a span of a few minutes, you should definitely reconsider moving forward with the conversation. Most people don't ask for money from a person they have just met online. If someone is asking for money, especially if it accompanies the threat of sending your private messages, photos or videos and communications immediately. Many of our sextortion victims were tricked into sending money via wire transfers to foreign countries or cryptocurrency. When presented with the threat of having their intimate images released or by being told a very convincing sob story about an ill family member or a pet. A catfisher's main goal is to communicate with you without you discovering their true identity, so they almost always have constant "technical issues" or they outright refuse to show their face or talk on the phone. Many of our catfishing or sextortion victims reported consistent technical issues when attempting to chat face to face, including broken cameras and microphones, only allowing text or direct messages, and using sexually explicit videos that seem prerecorded. Be wary if your love interests asked to move your conversation to a different messaging app like Google Hangouts, WhatsApp or Skype due to the technical issues. Chances are it's a trap. A quick way to check if the person you're chatting with has been using recycled photos is by conducting a reverse image search. This can be done by utilizing a reverse image tool on search engines like Google or premium facial recognition software like Pimeyes or Face Pinpoint. Keep in mind that these tools are generally limited to public photos and not content from private social media accounts. Before sending intimate images to them, try to get a picture of them doing or holding something specific, like a piece of paper with a code word on it. Also, avoid asking for pictures of their driver's license or passport as proof, because these are easy to forge and all you need is access to the Internet. Don't give out your personal information like your full name without being 100% certain that this person is real. Many catfishers will scour the Internet looking for romantic or familial ties that they can use to blackmail you later if your relationship turns sexual. Better yet, update your security settings on your social media platforms like Facebook, LinkedIn, and Twitter to provide minimal public information. If you have unfortunately found this video too late and have been the victim of a catfishing scheme gone wrong, don't panic, because you're not alone. Don't pay a ransom to the catfisher if they ask, as this will encourage them to ask for more money since you've already complied with their demands the first time. Document any and all evidence that you have with this person, and use it to file a report with the FBI's Internet Crimes Complaint Center. Lock down all of your social media profiles as much as you can, especially on platforms that tend to encourage public information like Twitter and LinkedIn. If you are a victim of sextortion and web blackmail, we recommend reaching out to an experienced sextortion attorney. Doing so will not only save you time, but future headaches and hassle. - What Are Some Reasons Why Someone Might Stop Attending Gamblers Anonymous? - Mind Over Substance: Kind: captions Language: en What are some reasons why someone might stop attending Gamblers Anonymous? Have you ever wondered why some people choose to stop attending Gamblers Anonymous meetings? This is a common question and understanding the reasons can help us support those in need. Let's take a closer look at some factors that might lead someone to step away from this important support group. First, some individuals may perceive their gambling issues as less severe than they really are. They might believe they can manage their gambling without ongoing help. This belief can reduce their motivation to keep attending meetings which stress the importance of total abstinence and commitment. Next, experiences with relapse can also play a significant role. If someone slips back into gambling and feels that the group reacts negatively or judgmentally, they may feel discouraged. This can lead to feelings of shame and isolation, prompting them to stop attending. Another reason could be the fit of the program with their specific gambling issues. Gamblers Anonymous tends to be more effective for those with severe gambling problems. Those with milder issues or frequent relapses might find the program less suitable, leading to higher dropout rates. Research shows that many individuals stop attending after their first meeting. The dropout rates can range from about 22% to over 40%. Additionally, less than 8% of people who start attending remain abstinate and active in the program after one year. This indicates that maintaining engagement can be quite challenging. Changes in meeting formats can also impact attendance. For instance, the shift from in-person meetings to online platforms like Zoom has caused some members to disengage. Some people may feel uncomfortable with technology or prefer face-to-face interactions leading them to stop attending. Moreover, the lack of additional support can hinder success in gamblers anonymous. Participation tends to improve when combined with other therapies or when family members are involved in related support groups. Without these extra supports, some members may struggle to maintain attendance and abstinence. Demographic factors can also play a role. Gamblers Anonymous has historically been maledominated, which may not fully address issues that are more relevant to women. This lack of focus can make the program less appealing and effective for some women, contributing to dropout rates. Lastly, the commitment to attend meetings is central to the gamblers's anonymous experience. When members fail to uphold this commitment due to personal challenges or lack of motivation, they may stop attending altogether. In summary, reasons for stopping attendance at Gamblers Anonymous can include personal beliefs about gambling control, experiences with relapse, program fit, changes in meeting formats, lack of additional support, and demographic factors. Understanding these reasons can help improve retention and recovery outcomes for those facing gambling addiction.

- What Is Families Anonymous? - Mind Over Substance: Kind: captions Language: en What is Families Anonymous? Have you ever felt overwhelmed by a loved one's struggles with addiction? If so, you are not alone. Families Anonymous is here to help. This organization is a 12step fellowship designed specifically for family members and friends of individuals dealing with substance abuse or related behavioral issues. Founded in 1971 by a group of parents in California, Families Anonymous has grown into an international network with hundreds of meetings held weekly across various countries. The main goal of Families Anonymous is to create a supportive community. Here, those affected by a loved one's addiction can share their experiences and find understanding. Members include parents, grandparents, siblings, spouses, and friends of individuals with current, suspected, or former drug or behavioral problems. This diverse group helps create a safe space for everyone involved. Families Anonymous follows a 12-step program similar to Alcoholics Anonymous. However, it is tailored to meet the needs of family and friends rather than focusing solely on the individual with the addiction. The 12 steps guide members through a healing process that addresses the emotional and psychological impact of living with someone who has addictive behaviors. Anonymity, mutual support, and education about addiction, and recovery are key components of this program. Meetings are free and open to anyone concerned about a loved one's substance use or related issues. Even if you suspect a problem, you are welcome to attend. Families Anonymous encourages a healthy environment by discouraging religious discussions, gossip, and excessive focus on the past. This approach helps maintain a constructive atmosphere for all participants. In addition to meetings, Families Anonymous offers literature and resources to help members understand addiction better. These materials provide guidance on coping with the effects of addiction on family dynamics. The program aims to help families avoid destructive patterns that can hinder their healing journey. It offers a path toward emotional recovery and improved relationships. Families Anonymous is a nonprofit support network designed to assist those affected by another person's addiction through shared experiences, education, and a structured 12step approach. It aims to promote emotional healing and resilience. If you are looking for support, consider reaching out to Families Anonymous. You may find the community and resources you need to navigate this challenging journey.

- The Psychology Behind Dangerous Social Media Understanding the Impact and Taking Action | Mental: Kind: captions Language: en the psychology behind dangerous social media understanding the impact and taking action psychology behind dangerous social media social media has become an integral part of our lives in recent times we use it to connect with friends and family stay updated with current events and share our experiences with others while social media has many benefits it also has a darker side dangerous social media can be defined as the use of social media to spread hate misinformation and propaganda this article will explore the psychology behind dangerous social media and how it affects our Behavior social identity Theory one of the most fundamental aspects of human psychology is social identity social identity Theory suggests that we derive our sense of self from the groups we belong to social media platforms provide us with a way to connect with like-minded individuals and form online communities based on shared interests or beliefs however these communities can also Foster a sense of group identity that can lead to dangerous Behavior in group bias is the tendency to favor members of our own group over others when we belong to an online community we are more likely to engage in behaviors that support our group's ideology even if it goes against our personal beliefs this is because we want to maintain our sense of belonging and avoid being ostracized by the group in-group bias can lead to dangerous behaviors such as cyber bullying harassment and hate speech confirmation bias confirmation bias is the tendency to seek out information that confirms our existing beliefs and ignore information that contradicts them social media algorithms are designed to show us content that is relevant to our interests which can create an echo chamber effect we are more likely to engage with content that supports our worldview and this can reinforce our beliefs even if they are false or harmful confirmation bias can also make us more susceptible to misinformation and propaganda conspiracy theories and fake news stories can spread rapidly on social media because they confirm people's existing beliefs this can lead to dangerous behaviors such as vaccine hesitancy political polarization and radicalization social comparison Theory social comparison Theory suggests that we evaluate our own worth based on how we compare to others way to compare ourselves to others on a massive scale we can see how many followers likes and comments other people have and compare ourselves to them social comparison can have both positive and negative effects on our Behavior on the one hand it can motivate us to improve ourselves and strive for success on the other hand it can lead to feelings of Envy jealousy and low self-esteem when we feel inferior to others we may engage in dangerous behaviors such as cyber bullying trolling or posting inflammatory content to get attention de-individuation the individuation is the phenomenon in which people lose their sense of individual identity and become more likely to engage in impulsive or aggressive behaviors this can happen when people feel Anonymous or part of a crowd social media platforms can create a sense of anonymity because people can hide behind fake profiles or Anonymous usernames when people feel Anonymous they are more likely to engage in behaviors that they would not do in person cyber bullying trolling and hate speech are all examples of de-individuation on social media the lack of consequences and the feeling of being part of a group can make people feel invulnerable and embolden them to engage in dangerous Behavior in conclusion the psychology behind dangerous social media benefits we must also be aware of its potential to harm us and others by understanding the psychology behind dangerous social media we can take steps to mitigate its negative effects and use social own biases and beliefs in being open to different perspectives we can also be more mindful of the content we consume and share online making sure that it comes from reputable sources and is not misleading or harmful another way to mitigate the negative effects of dangerous social media is to advocate for responsible use of social media this can include advocating for stronger regulations and policies to prevent hate speech and harassment on social media platforms it can also involve promoting digital literacy and critical thinking skills to help people recognize and avoid false information and propaganda finally we can also use social media to promote positive and constructive Behavior we can use our online presence to spread positivity promote diversity and inclusion and raise awareness about important social issues by using social media to connect with others and make a positive impact we can help counter the negative effects of dangerous social media can have serious consequences for individuals and Society by being more conscious of our own behavior online advocating for responsible use of social media and using social media to promote positivity and constructive Behavior we can help mitigate the negative effects of dangerous social media and create a safer more inclusive online community it's also important to note that social media platforms have a responsibility to address the negative effects of social media companies should take proactive steps to prevent the spread of hate speech misinformation and propaganda on their platforms this can include investing in content moderation and fact-checking systems implementing stricter Community guidelines and providing resources for users to report harmful content additionally social media companies should prioritize transparency and accountability in their operations users should have access to information about how their data is being used and who is profiting from it social media companies should also be accountable for the content that appears on their platforms especially when it promotes harmful Behavior in conclusion dangerous social media is a complex issue that requires a multifaceted approach dangerous social media individuals can take steps to mitigate its negative effects social media companies also have a responsibility to address the negative effects of dangerous social media through proactive measures and increase transparency and accountability ultimately creating a safer more inclusive online community requires the collective effort of individuals - The Horrors of Being a Facebook Moderator | Informer: Kind: captions Language: nl-NL Ik kwam binnen… …keek wat mijn doelstelling was voor die dag… …duwde op de knop, gewoon gaan. Daar is de eerste content, boem. Het staat op het scherm, voor je neus. Je kijkt, je neemt een beslissing… …je duwt op een paar knoppen, het verdwijnt en daar is de volgende. En de volgende. Je ziet dode lichamen en moorden. Mensen die vieren dat ze iemand hebben vermoord. Honden die levend worden gebarbecued... En je ziet die beelden. Het duurde lange tijd voor ik begreep hoe erg alles me had geraakt. Ik geloofde niet eens in PTSS. Ik dacht gewoon dat die mensen geen ruggengraat hadden. Het is moeilijk voor me, de zware emotionele reacties die ik voel… …als ik over die dingen praat. Ik ben in therapie gegaan... ...en had veel tijd nodig, om over mijn moeilijkheden heen te raken. Ik werkte voor Facebook. Het was mijn taak om acht uur per dag… …te kijken naar de ergste dingen die mensen online posten… …zodat jij ze niet te zien krijgt. Mark Zuckerberg had aangekondigd dat hij duizenden mensen wilde rekruteren… …om dit soort werk te doen. Ik denk dat ze simpelweg enorm veel mensen nodig hadden… …dus ik kreeg de baan. Ze vroegen me wel… …of ik begreep dat ik misschien zo nu en dan… …iets zou kunnen zien dat een beetje schokkend is. Ze vragen of je dat oké vindt. Maar je hebt er geen ervaring mee en begrijpt niet echt wat het betekent. Het is lastig te bepalen… …hoeveel beelden er van een bepaalde soort waren… …of hoe extreem het was. Je zou het kunnen kwantificeren, maar de cijfers zijn niet beschikbaar. Facebook heeft ze, maar als werknemer heb je niet het recht om te weten… …aan welke soort schadelijke beelden je bent blootgesteld. Ik vermoed dat vijf tot tien procent… …van wat je dagelijks ziet mogelijk traumatiserend is. Ik herinner me de eerste keer dat ik een soort wauw-moment had. Dat was toen we net begonnen waren. Ik kreeg op een dag een intens close-upbeeld… …van een man die masturbeerde. Maar ik wou niet naar voren reiken… …mijn hand in die ruimte brengen en de knoppen indrukken om het te deleten. Uiteindelijk deed een van de meisjes het en pakte ze… Er stonden natte doekjes… …op een ander bureau, om de tafel schoon te maken. Ze maakte de hele tafel schoon, het toetsenbord en haar eigen handen… …voor ze terugging naar haar bureau. Toen ik daar later over nadacht, besefte ik dat we werkelijk… …geraakt worden door wat we zien. Een paar weken later… …had één van mijn collega's zijn eerste zelfverwondingsincident. Iemand sneed zich in de pols… …en het leek erop dat die persoon zelfmoord zou plegen. We hoorden die man gillen en roepen… 'Shit. Wat moet ik doen? Ik heb een zelfmoord. Wat nu?' Hij was helemaal in paniek, want er stond een leven op het spel… …recht voor zijn neus, op dat exacte moment… …en het is je baan om mensen te beschermen. De richtlijnen zijn geen richtlijnen. Het zijn heel erg strikte, heel gedetailleerd uitgeschreven regels. We hebben definities… …van schade versus verwonding versus geweld. We hebben duidelijke definities van wat haatspraak is… …en wat geen haatspraak is. Je hoofd zwemt… …tussen al die problemen en dingen die je in overweging moet nemen… …telkens als je met dit soort dingen wordt geconfronteerd. Ik denk dat het makkelijker zou zijn om met de beelden om te gaan… …als je er niet zo diep over zou hoeven nadenken. Ik werkte in de avonddienst, dus ik begon om zes uur 's avonds. Ik was klaar om twee uur 's morgens. Maar drie, vier uur later werd ik vaak weer wakker. Dan zat ik plotseling recht in bed… …en herinnerde ik me een beslissing die ik had genomen. Ik realiseerde me dat ik een fout had gemaakt. Bijvoorbeeld, ik heb een tepel gemist. Je herinnert je een of ander beeld dat je hebt gezien. Plots realiseer je je dat er een naakt meisje was aan één kant… …of een ISIS-vlag in de achtergrond… …dus dat had eigenlijk verwijderd moeten worden, vanwege de terrorisme-regels. Je herhaalt natuurlijk alles in je hoofd… …onbewust, terwijl je slaapt. Maar je mag met niemand praten over je baan. We hebben allemaal een geheimhoudingsverklaring ondertekend… …de Facebook-omertà. Ze zeiden ons allemaal telkens opnieuw: 'Vertel nooit aan iemand wat je baan is'. Er was een welzijnsteam ter plaatse. Dat was verantwoordelijk voor weerbaarheidstraining… …en mentale gezondheidsproblemen. Maar het zijn geen gediplomeerde psychotherapeuten of psychologen. Geen mentale gezondheidsexperts. Tijdens je werk kreeg je dan een e-mail waarin stond… …'hallo, we gaan vanavond vingerverven in de kantine.' Ik zit hier om de wereld te redden. Ik kijk naar oorlogsmisdaden en kindermisbruik. Ik heb geen tijd om te gaan vingerverven. Het was schone schijn. De ondersteuning die er was, was ongeschikt en totaal niet toereikend. Bij mij kwam er een groeiend bewustzijn en een verlies van vertrouwen, veel hoop. Het werd uiteindelijk een saaie, hersenloze bezigheid… …waarin je jezelf ongevoelig had gemaakt voor wat je ziet… …op je scherm. Toen ik er wegging, was ik opgelucht. Ik werd gebeld door een journalist. Iemand had haar mijn nummer gegeven en ze wou met me praten. Vertrouwelijk, zoals hier, over mijn baan. Ze stelde me dezelfde vragen als jij, over de inhoud, de dingen die je ziet. Het was de eerste keer dat ik er iets mee deed… …dat ik het onder ogen zag. Ik werd er volledig door overrompeld en ik begon te huilen in een koffieshop. Ik was verloren. Ik wist gewoon niet wat ik deed wat er gebeurde. Ik was totaal verbijsterd. Toen ik het begon te begrijpen en met specialisten en dokters praatte… …besefte ik dat die baan er flink ingehakt had. Dat had ik me niet eens gerealiseerd. Facebook ontkent dat iemand ooit PTSS zou krijgen. 'Niemand werd geschaad.' Dat is erg expliciet. Er was ook een opname. Iemand heeft opgenomen wat Mark Zuckerberg zei… …tijdens een interne vergadering met werknemers vorig jaar. Mark Zuckerberg zei: 'Dat is een beetje melodramatisch.' Hij verwierp gewoon… …alle kritiek die er was geweest. Het is ontkenning. Ze willen niet weten wat er gebeurt. Ze zeggen de juiste dingen, het klinkt wel goed… …maar het interesseert hen geen bal. Het kan me niet schelen wat andere mensen aan Mark Zuckerberg hebben verteld. Deze verhalen zijn al zoveel keer herhaald. Hij moet op de werkvloer gaan kijken hoe de mensen daar elke dag werken. Hij moet zien wat er gebeurt en praten met mensen die werden geschaad… …en aanvaarden dat hij een probleem heeft dat hij moet oplossen.
