Child's support for Kayla took half my paycheck. The rest vanished like water poured into dry soil. Every week ended the same way with me staring at an empty checking account, knowing I'd need to swipe the credit cards again just to buy food. One night, I sat on my couch with three open beer cans on the coffee table and my phone glowing in my hand. I had been scrolling through pointless videos when an ad popped up about making money on Only Fans. I had always thought of it as something other people did. people who spent hours in the gym or lived in stylish apartments with ring lights and spotless countertops. But I wasn't in awful shape. Six feet tall, arms still solid from pulling transmissions and wrestling engine blocks. My body had that worn in look that came from years of physical work, not fitness routines. More than one of my ex's friends had called it rugged. Maybe that counted for something. Setting up the account felt unreal at first. I took photos in my bathroom mirror and tried not to think about the water stain on the ceiling behind me or the fact that the shower curtain was older than my marriage. The early photos were awkward, stiff, clumsy attempts at looking confident, but to my surprise, subscribers trickled in. 40 people in 2 weeks, each paying $15 a month. Not a fortune, but enough to fix the transmission on my truck and enough to make me think this might actually help. For the next 3 months, everything settled into a routine. I'd get home from the shop, wash the grease off my hands, and take a couple photos with decent lighting. Sometimes I recorded a short video on weekends. Most people who subscribed were polite, even kind, lonely people who wanted someone to notice them. They sent small tips and short messages telling me I looked good or that they liked my style. It didn't feel dirty or dangerous, just strange, like running a part-time job inside my phone. Then Goldeney 369 appeared at the end of June. No profile photo, nothing in the bio, just a plain username. But they tipped $200 the moment they joined without asking for anything in return. Over the next week, the tips kept coming. 100 here, 50 there. The messages were always vague compliments, nothing specific or personal. The money felt like found treasure. In two weeks, they sent almost $800, enough for me to catch up on utilities and plan a trip to Cedar Point for Kayla's upcoming 15th birthday. When they finally made a request, it came late on a humid Friday night in early July. A private video call for $500, 15 minutes, nothing complicated. They asked to use a platform called Secure Stream instead of the built-in video system, claiming they didn't trust the privacy settings on Only Fans. The site looked clean, professional, with a login page that felt almost corporate. I entered my email, tested my camera and microphone, and everything seemed normal enough. I didn't think about it too deeply. I needed the money, and the deadline for rent was looming like a shadow. I couldn't outrun. At 11:00 that night, I clicked the call link. My camera turned on. Their screen stayed black. They typed a message saying their camera was broken, but they could see me. The room around me felt warmer than usual. Maybe because the summer heat had been building all day. Maybe because my nerves were chewing at my stomach. For 15 minutes, I followed their instructions. I took off my shirt. I flexed a little. I told some generic story they asked for. I stood up, turned around, let them look at different angles. None of it felt invasive at the time. It just felt like work. The same kind of work I had been doing for months. When the timer hit 15 minutes, the call ended automatically. I stared at my reflection in the camera preview. Feeling a little silly, but also relieved and $500 richer, I went to bed without a second thought. The next morning, everything changed. I woke at 10 to my phone vibrating non-stop. 17 new emails from an address I didn't recognize. The subject line was the same on every single one, my name, and the words, "Open this now." My chest tightened even before I clicked the first message. Three attachments loaded slowly, each a crystal clear screenshot from the video call. My face sharp and unmistakable. My body angled in ways I wished I had never shown to a stranger. And worst of all, my full name visible in the corner from the secure stream account. The message below the images was short, demanding cold. They wanted $5,000 in Bitcoin by noon on Monday, or they would send everything to Jennifer, to my boss, to Kayla's school, to every single person on my Facebook friends list. At the bottom was a final line claiming they had recorded everything, including moments that made my stomach twist and my breath shortened just remembering them. The rest of Saturday blurred into a fog of panic. I checked my bank account again and again, even though I knew exactly how little was in it. I called my friend Craig, trying to ask vague questions about tracing emails and cryptocurrency. His answer made it clear the person who sent the threats knew exactly how to hide themselves. Each attachment was sharp enough to ruin my life in under a minute. I imagined those images spreading through the autoshop, through Kayla's school, through the neighborhood, through my ex's friend group. I imagined Kayla seeing them on someone's phone. Saturday and Sunday disappeared in a haze of humiliation and desperation. I sold everything I could. My PlayStation for a fraction of its worth. The Mustang engine block I'd been saving. I maxed out credit cards in a frantic series of cash advances. I even borrowed money from Doug, lying through my teeth about needing to repair my truck. By Sunday night, I sat at my kitchen table counting a stack of wrinkled bills and small transfer confirmations. I was still short. I emailed the blackmailer. Anyway, begging for more time. Their answer came minutes later. No exceptions. By noon Monday, I had scraped together the rest from a payday loan placed behind a liquor store. I initiated the Bitcoin transfer at 11:47 a.m. Feeling like I was watching my entire life slide out of reach. At 12:3 p.m., the confirmation arrived. Payment received. Your secret is safe for now. The words for now hollowed out my stomach. I knew the truth. People like that didn't just disappear after getting paid. For two weeks, I tried to rebuild the pieces of my life. Deleted the account. Changed my phone number and email. Picked up more hours at work. Tried convincing myself the worst was over. Then a kid showed up at the shop one Wednesday, barely 19, holding an envelope with my name on it. Inside was another screenshot, more explicit than the first batch, along with a second demand for $2,000. I knew I couldn't pay. I also knew I couldn't survive another round of this. So, I drove straight to the Columbus Police Department and asked for help. The detective who met me listened patiently, but everything she explained left me feeling smaller and smaller in the chair. Anonymous platforms, international servers, Bitcoin already moved through multiple countries. No jurisdiction, no way to identify the person. She told me my best option was to stop paying and document everything. maybe warn people before they were blindsided. The week after that stretched into something I can only describe as quiet terror. Nothing happened, but the silence felt heavier than the threats. Every time my phone buzzed, I jolted. Every unknown number made my pulse spike. Jennifer called about Kayla's schedule, and I nearly fainted, thinking she had seen something online, but nothing came. A week turned into two. Then three. I kept searching my own name online late at night, scrolling until my eyes burned. Nothing surfaced. Nothing leaked. The black mailer simply vanished into the same void they had come from. Four months have passed, long enough that the panic has dulled into something more permanent, a pressure that lives just beneath my ribs. I had to explain the financial mess to Jennifer eventually. Though I used a lie about gambling instead of telling her the truth, it was easier for her to believe I was irresponsible than something else. More manageable. She tightened the rules about when I could see Kayla and I didn't fight it because I felt like I had no right to anything anymore. I changed jobs to another auto shop two towns over, moved apartments, deleted every social media account. I started living like someone who didn't want to be found. The real horror didn't come from the blackmail itself. It came from what lingered afterward. The knowledge that someone somewhere had images of me saved on a hard drive or cloud folder. Images they could release at any time for any reason or for no reason at all. That lack of control sits with me every night when I try to fall asleep. When a stranger looks at me too long in a grocery store aisle. When an unfamiliar email hits my inbox and I freeze before opening it. A few days ago, a spam message landed in my junk folder about cryptocurrency investing. It had nothing to do with any of this, just a generic scam. But the moment I saw the words Bitcoin and anonymous in the subject line, my hands started shaking so violently that I had to sit down on the edge of my bathtub, bent over, trying not to throw up. It took me half an hour to calm down. That's the part no one warns you about. Once someone steals a private piece of your life, even for a moment, you never get it back. You walk around with the constant awareness that a version of you is floating out there in some dark corner of the internet waiting. Maybe the person who took it will never use it again. Maybe they've forgotten about me. Maybe they moved on to another victim and wiped their files clean. But maybe they didn't. And every day when I open my email or answer my phone or hear a notification buzz in my pocket, I brace myself for the next strike, the next demand, the next message that could break whatever fragile normal I've managed to rebuild. I keep moving anyway because I don't know what else to do. I go to work. I see Kayla when I can. I take long drives at night with the windows down, trying to feel like I'm still a person who exists outside the shadow of one terrible choice. I remind myself that nothing has happened in months. That silence might be a sign that I slipped through the cracks. But the truth is simple and crushing. The story didn't end when the threat stopped. It ended the moment I realized they could come back anytime without warning. And all I can do is hope that whoever held my life hostage once has let it drift so far into the background that they'll never bother to reach for it again. In the autumn of 2023, everything I thought I understood about the internet, relationships, and trust started to shift in ways I couldn't recognize at first. My senior year of engineering at a state university in Michigan should have been about long nights in the lab, job applications, and figuring out what life would look like once I crossed the stage in the spring. I had a small off-campus apartment, cheap furniture, bought secondhand off Facebook Marketplace, [music] and a routine that felt predictable enough to be comforting. Brooke had always been part of that routine. We met sophomore year and eventually lived together in that cramped apartment until her nursing program schedule made a longer commute impossible. She moved into her own place closer to the hospital. We still spent most weekends together, still met up in the campus coffee shop whenever our schedules matched and still talked about the kind of future that felt close enough to touch. When she told me in September that she planned to start an Only Fans account to help with her student loans, I tried to process it the way she framed it. She made it sound manageable. Simple content that wouldn't push boundaries. She had always been practical about money, and the debt hanging over her had been a constant source of stress for months. I didn't want to be the reason she stayed financially stuck. So, I told myself it was fine. She seemed lighter the first few weeks after starting. She bought small things she'd been putting off for ages, like better lighting equipment, and even surprised me with takeout after months of cheap pasta and instant soup. Whenever her phone buzzed with subscriber notifications, she would get this mix of excitement and relief that felt contagious. I wanted to believe everything was going smoothly. By early November, the changes were subtle enough that I brushed them aside at first. Shorter replies, longer gaps between messages, missed study sessions that used to be the highlight of long days. When we did meet up, she kept checking her phone, her face tightening in ways I hadn't seen before. Her clothing changed, too. Long sleeves pulled over her hands, even in overheated buildings. She started moving like someone who was always bracing for something, shoulders slightly raised, eyes darting to whatever sound happened around us. I asked if the online work was stressing her, but she just brushed it off, insisting she was fine. The way she forced the explanation made something twist in my stomach, but I didn't know what to do with that feeling. The breaking point came in mid November. She didn't respond to my messages for two full days. She had never disappeared like that, not even during her toughest exam stretches. I went to her apartment more than once, each time hoping she'd open the door with an apology about being tired or overwhelmed. Instead, her roommate said she hadn't seen Brooke in days, only heard her door once or twice at odd hours. I tried to convince myself maybe she was visiting her family or studying with classmates, but the pit in my stomach grew heavier by the hour. That Friday, I skipped my thermodynamics lecture and drove straight to her building. The front security door didn't latch properly unless someone slammed it, so I got inside easily and climbed to the third floor. I paused outside her apartment when I heard faint noise inside. It sounded like a video, maybe music layered under a soft voice. I knocked for minutes. My voice echoed in the hallway as I called her name, trying not to imagine anything too extreme. When the handle turned under my hand and the door swung open, the apartment was dark and still. No sign of her, but her laptop sat open on the kitchen counter, the screen casting a harsh blue light across the empty room. What I saw froze me on the spot. Her only fan streaming dashboard was open, still logged in, showing an active live stream with hundreds of viewers. On the screen, Brooke sat in a room I didn't recognize. Bare white walls, a mattress on the floor, fluorescent lighting that washed all color out of her skin. She was talking into the camera, but her voice didn't sound like hers. It was too bright, too controlled, almost recited. Her eyes kept shifting towards something offscreen, and every few seconds she paused as if waiting for someone to cue her. The chat was filling with comments, tips, requests, but she wasn't reacting. I had watched some of her early streams to be supportive, and she had always been relaxed, playful, present. This version of her felt like watching a stranger wearing her face. I grabbed the laptop and sat on the floor trying to swallow the rising panic. As the stream continued, I noticed small things that made everything worse. Faint red marks around her wrists when she lifted her arms. Tightness in her jaw that made her smile look painful. Rapid blinking patterns that looked too intentional to be random. I used my phone to screen record everything while digging through the dashboard settings, searching for any clue about where the stream originated. The IP address kept shifting, bouncing across Michigan cities every few minutes. Her recent uploads showed daily content filmed in that same Stark room, each one more mechanical than the last. A withdrawal account had been changed to a crypto wallet 3 days earlier. The numbers on the tips were far higher than anything she'd mentioned. None of it made sense. I called 911 with my voice shaking so badly I could barely explain what I'd seen. They redirected me and transferred me until someone finally took the report without any urgency. They said she couldn't officially be considered missing yet, and the fact that she was still posting content suggested everything was voluntary. I drove to the police station with the laptop, hoping someone would take it seriously. The desk sergeant barely glanced at the screen before dismissing it as a relationship dispute or a misunderstanding about adult content work. Even when I pointed out the marks on her wrists and the strange movements, I was told people did all kinds of things online for higher earnings. They took my name and sent me away. For the next 3 weeks, finding her became my entire life. I abandoned everything else. My assignments, my job applications, even basic sleep. I contacted everyone who knew her. Her parents hadn't heard from her, but didn't seem alarmed, saying she often got caught up in school. I stayed up all night analyzing recorded streams, mapping timestamps, isolating background sounds. Sometimes I heard footsteps or a door closing. Once the muffled rumble of a train, I built folders full of screenshots, audio files, transcriptions of her movements, anything that looked like a pattern. I reached out to private investigators and spent money I didn't have. One traced the crypto withdrawal path, but hit a wall within days. In early December, the streams abruptly stopped. Her profile went silent. The absence of new content terrified me more than the videos themselves. I kept checking the site every hour. Unable to shake the fear that something irreversible had happened. I searched hospital records. My friend had access to [music] missing person's lists, even Jane do entries. Each time I scrolled through those descriptions, something in me cracked a little more. Her apartment was soon cleared out. I went through her belongings before the landlord threw them into a dumpster. Textbooks, clothes, cosmetics, a half finishedish knitting project she'd been working on for her mother. Nothing unusual. Her roommate later mentioned mysterious packages Brooke had received in the weeks before she disappeared. Boxes she carried straight to her room with the labels torn off before they reached the recycling. It added another layer to the uncertainty, another unanswered question. Months passed with no progress until late February 2024 when her Only Fans account suddenly posted again. The notification hit my phone at 3:00 in the morning. The new video showed her thinner, her face sharper around the edges, her hair dyed black and cut short. She wore a necklace I had given her, something I had seen in her jewelry box when I went through her room. She moved strangely, like each motion had been rehearsed. Her shoulders had faint scars that hadn't existed before. The room was different now, still bleak, but featuring a window reflecting city lights. She never spoke. She simply performed while occasionally glancing toward the camera with a hollow expression that felt like a plea without words. At one point, she turned and in the window reflection, I saw a tall figure standing perfectly still near a door. I replayed the video over and over, looking for anything I could use. Then I noticed her tapping her fingers against her thigh in a rhythm. I slowed the footage down to half speed, then quarter speed. After hours of comparing patterns, I realized she was signaling numbers. It took until dawn, but the sequence resembled an address. Not exact, but close enough to narrow down a section of Detroit. I drove there that morning, exhausted, but wired with fear. The area had old industrial buildings, some converted into lofts, others left abandoned. I walked for hours, comparing window layouts to the angles in the video. Near sunset, I found a building that matched. The same window placement, the same view of distant city lights. I waited outside until someone left, slipped in before the door shut, and climbed to the fourth floor. Music drifted from the corner unit, the same bland electronic beat from her recent videos. I called the police again, giving them only the address and claiming to have heard dangerous noises. They arrived slowly and looked irritated. They knocked and waited. Everything inside went silent. When someone finally opened the door, a man insisted nothing was wrong and that he lived alone. The officers didn't push. They didn't check the unit. They didn't question anything. As they escorted me away, I glanced back and saw a silhouette at the window with a hand pressed against the glass before being pulled into darkness. I refused to stop. I filed reports with national agencies and reached out to journalists. Most didn't respond. The few who did said there wasn't enough evidence. I hired another investigator, someone with experience in trafficking cases. She uncovered a network of shell companies tied to dozens of similar apartments across the Midwest, all used for cam content operations. She told me Brooke was very likely part of something larger that without official cooperation, the chances of extracting anyone were incredibly small. She advised me to continue documenting, but warned that pushing too hard could make things worse for the victims. The streams appeared occasionally over the next few weeks, each one showing her more drained, more detached, as if whatever inner resistance she had was slipping. The last one came in April. She looked straight at the camera with a clarity I hadn't seen in months. Her expression was empty, but aware. She didn't speak, but the way she stared conveyed a message I understood instantly. She wanted me to stop watching, to stop feeding a system that thrived on attention. I closed my laptop and stepped away. I sent every piece of evidence I had collected to multiple law enforcement agencies, and then forced myself to disconnect. I finished my degree in May, though most of that semester exists as a blur. Sometimes I still check missing person's databases, unable to fully let go. Her account remains active, or so I've been told. I keep my promise not to look. There are nights when I wake up with the image of her hand against that window burned into my mind, wondering if I missed something. If there was a call I didn't make soon enough, or if I had clung too tightly to the idea that someone would eventually take it seriously. All I know is that she is out there somewhere living under the control of people who hide in the open. And even though I stepped back, the truth of what happened continues to shape everything I see, everything I trust. And everything I allow myself to believe about the world we live in. Phoenix in July was unbearable. The heat pressed against my tiny studio apartment near Camelback Road like a living thing, thick and unrelenting. And the air conditioning, if you could even call it that, barely whispered. It rattled and wheezed like it was struggling under the weight of its own uselessness. And by midafter afternoon, the room was already suffocating. Brandon had been gone for 3 weeks. Yet, remnants of him still clung to the space in ways that made me feel like I hadn't really left him behind at all. A phone charger appeared behind the nightstand as if it had sprouted there overnight. His favorite hot sauce sat in the back of the fridge, and one of his socks, limp and damp, had somehow adhered itself to the inside of the dryer. Moving out had been an exercise in urgency and heartbreak. My sister Grace had helped me. Stuffing my entire life into her pickup truck in under two hours, while I stumbled through the process like I was already hollowed out. 4 years together and leaving felt like ripping off my own skin. The locks were changed the same day I moved in. Every notification on my phone made my chest tighten. A reflexive fear that it might be him. Somehow breaking through blocks and restrictions, demanding attention, demanding a control. That dread lived in the corners of every room. Creating an Only Fans account wasn't something I'd planned. It had been Melody, my friend, who had convinced me, showing screenshots of her own success, laughing over the numbers that dwarfed her nursing salary. I had set up my profile with a mix of curiosity, defiance, and a small, almost shameful thrill. Sitting cross-legged on my secondhand couch, laptop warm against my thighs, I thought about Brandon and how he would have reacted. That thought made me smile for the first time in weeks. A real smile, one that belonged entirely to me. The first uploads were cautious lingery against the exposed brick of my bedroom wall. Angles and lighting that Melody had helped me perfect. Nothing too revealing. Within 3 days, I had 40 subscribers. By the end of the second week, [music] the income covered half my rent. There was a liberation in it, a sense of autonomy that had been missing for far too long. But the freedom came with an undertoe. It started on a Wednesday morning, a comment from a username I hadn't seen before, Desert Watcher 62. At first, it was just an observation, a compliment almost. The way I bit my lip when nervous. It made my stomach twist. That habit, something Brandon had noticed endlessly, something intimate, personal, familiar. I tried to convince myself it was coincidence that lots of strangers on the internet notice little details. Then the comments became more precise. A birthark on my hip, a corner of my mattress visible in a photo, the side I slept on. Each message was a needle of recognition, a scratch on skin only I felt only I could see. The specificity grew with every post. notes about a red dress I wore in Sedona, mentions of the exact hour I looked best, according to him, small reminders of fights that lasted until dawn, words of praise and cruelty folded into memory. There was nothing threatening in the traditional sense. No explicit threats, no demands, no illegal language, but every line, every remark carried a weight of possession, a reminder that someone knew me, had known me, and had not let go. It was slow. It was patient. And it burrowed under my skin. It escalated quickly. Emails appeared sent to my father's work account, my mother's Gmail, my sister's office email. Screenshots from my Only Fans page. Mild, tame images annotated with circles and arrows pointing out scars, moles, and other intimate details. The most disturbing contained a photo I had never posted, taken in my bathroom mirror, lingery on, my face fully visible. Whoever had access to that had access to me in ways that felt impossible and frightening. It became clear I was dealing with someone who knew my life, knew my body, and knew my history intimately. I went to the police. The Phoenix Police Department on Washington Street smelled faintly of bleach and had a sticky quality that made the plastic chairs cling to my thighs. Officer Garrett typed slowly, methodically, eyes not lifting from the screen as he asked questions. My proof was circumstantial commentary from posts that only someone with deep knowledge could have written. The officer pulled up Brandon's record, [music] his past harassment charge, and a probation officer confirming his self, reported whereabouts didn't match surveillance evidence. Security footage revealed him parked across from my apartment on multiple nights. Hours spent watching, always leaving exactly after 3 hours. It was real. It was undeniable. A temporary restraining order felt like the first breath of clean air in weeks. Seeing the judge sign it, watching Brandon's jaw clench in the courtroom as his lawyer tried to negotiate excuses was both relieving and frightening. He had been close, and the closeness still lingered in shadows and digital footprints. Even after the legal protections were in place, new accounts appeared on my posts. Sunset driver 88, Phoenix native 45, Valley resident 77. each a small invasive reminder that someone might still be watching. Observing the furniture I replaced, the wine I displayed, the choices I made, the names changed, the IP addresses varied, but the tone, the obsessive knowledge stayed the same. I moved to a new apartment in Scottsdale, installing multiple locks, security cameras, and even a German Shepherd named Bruno. The heat was still unbearable in July. The air conditioning still a struggle, but the paranoia remained. Every new comment, every benign message is now filtered through a lens of suspicion. The world outside my apartment feels almost too ordinary, almost too safe. And yet there's an invisible hand that reminds me, a subtle pressure, a history that refuses to dissolve. The digital footprints he left behind, the pattern of obsession lingered, teaching me that autonomy in a connected world comes with its own vulnerabilities. Even now, months later, I check the comments carefully, wary of usernames that might conceal his presence. Every post is a negotiation between freedom and caution. Every small smile I allow myself is tinged with memory. I have protection, the law on my side, and a physical space fortified against intrusion. But some part of me knows that the awareness of being watched, even after the danger is mostly controlled, is an experience that cannot be erased. That tension, that reminder of my own boundaries violated stays in the corners of the rooms I inhabit. I have my life back. My career on Only Fans growing steadily. The income to match independence. And yet the experience has reshaped my sense of privacy, of safety, and of trust. The lesson is permanent freedom is not only earned, but fiercely guarded. And the smallest oversight can open doors to the most intimate invasions. Even with cameras, locks, and Bruno, I still feel it sometimes. The faint, almost imperceptible weight of someone knowing everything about me. Every new subscriber brings a flicker of caution. Every upload, every post is a reminder that the past can reach forward in ways that are silent but unmistakable. And yet, I am no longer the same person who sat on a secondhand couch, trembling with both excitement and fear, thinking about what Brandon would say. I am someone who has fought, survived, and reclaimed autonomy in a world where exposure is a risk and privacy a prize. I breathe differently now. I act with care, but with certainty. I live cautiously, freely, and fully, knowing what it takes to protect oneself, and knowing that sometimes the real horror isn't supernatural. It's entirely achingly human. Everyone in Still Water knew me as the girl who made the perfect oat milk lad at Common Grounds. That little coffee shop on Main Street with the exposed brick walls and hanging plants everywhere felt like my entire world. I had been working there for 2 years, ever since I dropped out of college. Saving money while taking online accounting classes. The pay was barely enough to cover rent for my tiny studio apartment above the antique shop, but the tips kept me afloat, and the regulars felt like family. Mornings were my favorite part of the day. I would unlock the door at 6, hear the espresso machine start its hiss, and watch the town wake through the big front windows as the first batch of muffins filled the air with warmth. Life was simple and predictable in the most comforting way. Just me and my cat, Oliver. our little routine keeping the world at a safe distance. Nobody knew about my only fans account except my best friend Ashley who lived in Dallas. And that was exactly how I wanted it. My mom thought I was saving myself for marriage and half the town probably assumed the same about the quiet barista in oversized sweaters who kept to herself. The account began as a joke after a Friday night video call with Ashley fueled by too much wine. She had been posting mostly feet pictures and occasional lingery shots and had been making enough to pay off her student loans. I figured trying it for a month could help me cover Christmas gifts and maybe finally fix the rattling noise in my Toyota Camry. I picked the username Coffee Bean Queen because it felt clever and tied to my day job without being obvious. I never showed my face, just my tattoos, a moon phase down my spine, and a tiny birthark on my hip that looked like a butterfly. Within 3 days, the account exploded. Thousands of followers, far more than Ashley had ever gotten. The money started coming in faster than I could process. By the end of the first week, I had made more than my monthly salary at the coffee shop. Posting these pictures felt tame compared to most creators, but the sudden attention was intoxicating. I kept telling myself it was harmless, just a little experiment for extra cash. The first sign something was wrong came on a Wednesday morning in late October. Three men I had never seen before came into Common Grounds, ordered plain black coffees, and sat together in the corner booth. They spent over 2 hours there, glancing in my direction repeatedly and whispering to each other. One had his phone angled at me the entire time. I told myself it was a coincidence. Maybe they were talking about coffee or something trivial, but a tight knot formed in my chest. An unease I couldn't shake. The following days were worse. New faces appeared regularly, always men, always in groups, always lingering, always watching. Some pretended to read newspapers, clearly taking photos. Others had the audacity to point their phones at me as I bent down to get supplies. Even the regulars noticed. Harold, a man who had been coming in every morning since his wife died, asked if the newcomers were bothering me, but I couldn't explain that strangers from the internet were stalking me because of pictures they had seen online. I smiled and said they were probably just passing through, though deep down I knew that wasn't true. Nobody passed through still water without reason. By the second week of November, it had reached a terrifying level. Every shift felt like a performance. Every movement scrutinized. Payments became messages. Men left bills larger than their order, scrolled with phrases that ranged from desperate to threatening. A man in a cowboy's jersey asked in a way that made my skin crawl if I did private shows. Keith, my manager, caught a group following me to my car after closing and never left my side again. Always hovering near the bat he kept behind the counter. The fear that had settled into my chest became a constant weight. I dreaded walking to work, carrying myself as if every step was under surveillance. The breaking point arrived on a Monday night when I found an envelope under my windshield wiper. Inside were screenshots from my Only Fans account, the moon phase tattoo clearly visible, along with a note that stated they knew where I lived, accompanied by a photo of my apartment building. My hands went numb, the parking lot spinning around me as the flickering street light cast shadows that seemed to mock me. That night, I deleted everything. The account, the associated Instagram, the payment methods, all gone. I thought this would end it, that they would move on once I stopped feeding their attention. I was wrong. The harassment intensified. The men at the coffee shop became more aggressive, demanding explanations for why I had deleted my account, as if stalking a person at work were reasonable behavior. Their messages escalated from desperation to threats. Keith installed security cameras and kept the police non-emergency number taped to the register. But the legal system felt powerless against this careful calculating harassment. When groups of men showed up wearing matching red baseball caps embroidered with CBQ, even the police seemed reluctant to intervene. Their words implied that unless I was physically harmed, the law couldn't touch them. Detective Mills initially offered hope. She listened, took notes, promised to review footage, interview suspects, and increase patrols near my apartment. But the hope faded. A few days later, her tone changed. She spoke as though coached, warning me not to escalate. That was when I learned the truth. One of the men in the red caps was Jordan Hutcherson, the sheriff's youngest son, long protected by his father's influence. Fear turned to rage. These were not random strangers from the internet. This was a privileged group exploiting the system to torment me. I documented everything obsessively, saving photos, recording license plates, and storing security footage. Keith and Harold formed an informal protection network, taking shifts at the shop. Ashley drove down from Dallas to stay with me. My neighbors offered support, letting their dog patrol the complex when I heard suspicious footsteps. The world felt like a maze where everyone knew the rules except me and the stakes were life and sanity. The final escalation occurred the Saturday night after Thanksgiving. I woke to Oliver hissing at my bedroom window. His fur bristled, eyes wide, and outside I saw silhouettes on the fire escape. Two more with one holding a sign in bold red letters that said last chance. My body froze. The men knew exactly where I slept and had climbed to my second floor window without any fear of consequence. I called Keith and he arrived with Harold and three other regulars armed with flashlights and bats. Chasing the shadows away, I packed my belongings, everything I could carry, and left still water at first light. Oliver crying in his carrier, the town disappearing behind me. Keith wired me my last paycheck plus a bonus. and Harold slipped $500 into my car window despite my protests. I drove away, leaving behind my job, my apartment, and any sense of safety. Eight months later, I sit in a city I do not name under a new name. My hair dyed black, working a job far removed from coffee. I considered tattoo removal for the moon phases down my spine, but have not gone through with it, unwilling to give them that final victory. Occasionally, [music] I Google Jordan Hutcherson, wondering if he still roams still water in his red cap, praying on someone else's life. Keith keeps me updated through burner emails, fabricating stories about my location to ditter further pursuit. The money I earned from that brief period on Only Fans remains untouched. A constant reminder of what it cost me, my home, my peace of mind, my trust in the world. I still wake at 2:00 in the morning, expecting to see shadows outside my window. And Oliver still hisses at figures that may no longer exist. But I am alive and alive in a way I never thought possible. Having escaped a nightmare built on obsession and privilege. I have rebuilt myself slowly and carefully. And while fear lingers, I have a life again, one that belongs entirely to me. It is the only ending I could hope for. A clear, hard one survival grounded in reality and entirely my own. Chicago in late spring always carried this kind of restless energy like the city itself was waking up after a long winter and daring people to chase something bigger than themselves. I had been living in my small Wicker Park apartment for over a year, renting the top floor of a crumbling brick building with a neighbor who practiced drums at all hours and a fire escape that shook every time a bus rolled by on the street below. At 24, I had finally begun to see some momentum in my Only Fans career after months of grinding, experimenting with content, scheduling posts, and learning which strategies worked for my audience. I spent hours editing, organizing, and planning. And when I got a message from Valentina Rose on Instagram one Wednesday afternoon, it felt like the kind of opportunity that could actually change everything. Her account had the blue verification check mark. Her posts were polished and current, and her story indicated she was actively collaborating with Chicago creators that week. She claimed she had been following me for months and wanted to do a collaboration shoot to expand both our audiences. The Airbnb she had booked was 40 minutes outside the city in one of those wealthy suburbs where every house sat on acres of manicured lawns distant from any neighbor and shielded by thick trees. The description in her messages made the house sound like a dream for content creation floor, tip ceiling windows, natural light flooding the main rooms, a clean, modern aesthetic that made everything look effortless. I arrived early in the afternoon, pulling up to a massive house that looked like it had been lifted straight from an architecture magazine. The driveway was empty. The front door was unlocked, just as the messages had said. And the interior matched the description almost too perfectly. Marble counters, a huge sectional couch, and professional ring lights already set up in what appeared to be the main shooting area. There was a chemical smell that lingered in the air, faint, but unusual, like a mix of cleaning agents and something more metallic. I unpacked my outfits, laying everything carefully on the bed of one of the bedrooms, trying to focus on arranging colors and textures while the creeping anxiety in my chest grew heavier by the minute. About 20 minutes after I arrived, voices drifted from downstairs. Relief washed over me, thinking that Valentina had arrived with her photographer or assistant. But when I reached the living room, two men I had never seen before were there. One tall and lanky with a scraggly beard introduced himself as Marcus and the other shorter stockier with thick glasses named Phil. Both wore matching black polo shirts with a production company logo I didn't recognize. Marcus explained that Valentina had sent them ahead to set everything up, that she was running late from another shoot, but would be there within the hour. Phil was already adjusting ring lights and checking camera angles with his phone. They presented themselves as professional and knowledgeable, referencing Valentina's content and their work on her past collaborations. They knew specifics about my content as well, referencing posts I had made weeks ago and even mentioning some of my subscribers by username. Their familiarity at first made me feel like I could trust them, like everything had been planned and organized to make me feel safe. Marcus pulled out an iPad and showed a presentation detailing the production company's previous collaborations, distribution deals, and how they could help expand my audience. He highlighted numbers, engagement statistics, and opportunities that were hard to argue with. Phil moved around the room, setting up additional cameras, locking the front door for privacy, and talking about technical details that sounded plausible. Then Marcus presented a contract 15 or 20 pages long with dense legal language about content rights, distribution terms, perpetual licensing, and derivative works. I paused, feeling the weight of my gut instinct telling me that something was off. When I suggested taking time to read the document carefully or have someone review it first, Marcus and Phil began applying pressure. Every word they said felt choreographed, emphasizing lost opportunity, missed deadlines, and how many other creators were waiting. The way they leaned, moved, and spoke over each other created a tension that left me disoriented. My chest tightened, my hands shook slightly, and the house that had seemed perfect minutes ago now felt like a trap. I excused myself to the bathroom to think, keeping the door locked, trying to get a signal to reach Valentina. I texted her directly, but the messages wouldn't send. My phone showed full bars, yet no messages were going through. When I attempted to check her Instagram profile again, it suddenly displayed as oozer not found. Even though I had just been messaging with her hours earlier, my pulse raced as the chemical smell from downstairs seemed to thicken in the air, making the space feel tighter, more claustrophobic. I heard low murmurss from Marcus and Phil outside the door. something about equipment in the van, and my instincts screamed at me to leave immediately. I left the bathroom with my phone in hand, pretending to have received an urgent call about a water leak in my apartment. Marcus's expression shifted immediately, the friendly veneer dropping into something sharper, and Phil moved closer to block the door, filming as if it were necessary for test footage. The configuration of the room, the way they stood, the weight of the contract on the table, it all felt like a deliberate effort to corner me to leave no room for escape. I held my phone up to my ear, pretending to talk to my roommate, describing the exact location, fabricating details about emergency management and landlord notifications, knowing that any hesitation could be dangerous. The brief distraction was enough to give me a small opening, I moved quickly toward the exit, walking as if I were still on the call and managed to reach the door. I yanked it open and ran toward my car, my keys thankfully still in my jacket pocket. The drive back to Chicago was a blur. My adrenaline stayed high the entire way as I checked my mirrors, anticipating that someone might be following. I finally stopped in a Walgreens parking lot to collect my thoughts and check my phone. Notifications flooded in. My messages from my roommate piled up. And when I checked Instagram, the original message thread from Valentina Rose was gone. Her verified account had reappeared, showing posts from 3 days earlier in Miami, nowhere near Chicago. When I reached out through direct messages, the real Valentina replied within minutes, explaining that she had no knowledge of the supposed collaboration and that a verified account hack had likely been used to set up the fake meeting. A few days later, the scale of the situation became terrifyingly clear. My best friend, Alexis, called, frantic, insisting I check my computer immediately. Someone had uploaded seven videos to multiple adult sites, all featuring AI generated content that replicated my face, body type, and distinctive features. The videos were entirely fabricated, showing scenarios I had never participated in in rooms I had never entered with people I had never met. Even small details like the birthark on my shoulder and the way I styled my hair were mirrored perfectly. Some of my actual subscribers had already found the videos and sent messages asking if I had started posting more extreme content. The videos kept appearing on new sites despite every takedown request and the platforms offered limited assistance. Weeks of investigation revealed that this was not an isolated incident. Other creators across the Midwest had received similar collaboration invites from hacked verified accounts. Pressured into signing contracts and sometimes had deep fake content uploaded after refusing to cooperate, we connected privately, sharing our experiences and discovering patterns. The Airbnbs had all been expensive rentals paid with stolen credit cards. The men used different names, but looked identical across incidents. The chemical smell that had unnerved me in the house was likely a signal jammer designed to block phone and GPS signals inside the properties. With the help of a cyber security expert one of the creators knew, we traced the deep fake videos to a Telegram group with thousands of members trading and selling custom AI content for cryptocurrency. Guides for targeting creators hacking accounts and producing realistic deep [music] fake videos were openly shared in the group. Months of collaboration with the FBI's cyber crime division led to the arrest of six individuals running the operation across three states. But the psychological and professional damage was already done. Even now, messages occasionally appear from people who have seen the deep fakes, reminding me that once something exists online, it never fully disappears. The experience left me hyper aware, careful, and skeptical of seemingly perfect opportunities. But it also strengthened my resilience. I learned to trust instincts over appearances, verify everything personally, and build community with other creators who could share knowledge and warn each other of threats. It was a long, exhausting process, but in the end, I reclaimed control over my work and my identity, even though the scars of exposure and manipulation remain as a cautionary reminder of how quickly ambition and trust can be exploited in the digital age. I reached a point in my life when everything felt like it was collapsing at once. And there was no dramatic moment that triggered it. Just the slow grind of bills, shifts, and exhaustion that kept building until it felt like the walls of my own life were shrinking. My part time barista job barely paid for anything. And the small apartment I shared with Clare on Lian Street felt even smaller when both of us were home. Moving around each other like we were living inside a storage closet. The place always smelled faintly like old wood and reheated noodles because the kitchen vents didn't work properly. Clare worked nights at a bookstore and slept through the day. Her schedule always opposite mine. We barely saw each other except in passing. Two tired people drifting through the same cramped hallway. The reminder for my student debt arrived in the mail one morning. A thick envelope with numbers that felt impossible, like they belonged to someone not even alive yet. I sat on the couch staring at it while the light crept across the carpet and something inside me just sag. I realized I needed another source of income, something immediate, something that didn't require commuting or a new skill or anything complicated. The idea that came to me felt ridiculous. selling feet photos online. I remembered laughing at the concept months earlier, thinking nobody would pay for something so absurd. But after I thought about it for a while, it started to feel like an option that wasn't as wild as it sounded. I created a username, kept everything anonymous, and started with a few photos taken on my phone. Nothing special, just simple shots. The anonymity felt like a shield. No face, no name, no real details. I kept the page minimal and avoided any personal information. The first few days were quiet and then the notifications started. A few subscribers here and there, polite usernames, small tips that added up quicker than I expected. It was strange but functional and the extra income covered groceries and part of the electricity bill. It felt like cheating at real life in a way, an unexpected loophole that I benefited from without thinking too much about. There was one account that stood out because it had a pattern of being steady without being intrusive. The username was gentle steps. His messages were simple compliments, never pushing for anything beyond what was already posted. I never gave him more attention than anyone else. But I noticed his username often because it popped up regularly in my notifications. It felt harmless. The flowers arrived on a quiet afternoon in early autumn. I found them propped against the door when I came home from the cafe. A tall bouquet of white liies wrapped in soft paper. They were my favorite, but I had never mentioned that anywhere. Inside was a small card with only my username written in elegant handwriting. There was no sender name, no clues at all. I placed the bouquet on the counter, thinking maybe Clare had done it for fun, but when she came home, she had no idea what I was talking about. I left the liies where they were, bright against the empty kitchen, trying not to think too hard about how someone had found my address. 2 days later, another delivery arrived. This time, it was a small box. Inside was a keychain shaped like a small foot. The object itself wasn't threatening, but the way it was presented made something cold settle inside my spine. The box had no return address, no note, nothing but my apartment number written with the same neat precision I'd seen on the card. I began to lose the ability to sleep properly. Every sound from the hallway made me tense. The hum of the refrigerator at night felt louder than usual. The shadows in the corners of the room seemed to stay longer than they should. Clare tried to reassure me that it was probably just a mistake or some kind of mixup. But the keychain felt intentional in a way I couldn't explain. I started keeping my phone close, checking the PO more than I needed to, staring at the door at night as though expecting it to rattle. I stopped feeling safe inside the apartment, and the feeling got worse every day. A week after the keychain, I came home one evening and the hallway light outside the apartment door flickered in a slow, irregular pattern, casting shadows that stretched across the floor. Clareire was out working her shift, and the apartment felt heavy when I walked in, almost like the air had thickened. The liies on the counter had begun to rot. Their petals were browning at the edges, curling inward, and the sweet smell clung to the air in a way that made my stomach turn. I reached for them to throw them away, and that was when I heard it. A soft scrape at the front door. The sound was faint, but deliberate. Metal brushing against metal. I stiffened, frozen in place. The sound came again closer to the lock. I could feel the pressure in my chest tighten as I stood there unable to move. The scraping stopped after a few seconds, followed by slow footsteps retreating down the hallway. When Clare arrived later, we looked through the pee hole but saw nothing. The corridor was empty, the light still flickering as though struggling to stay alive. The next morning, when I opened the door to leave for work, I saw them. Dozens of printed copies of my own photos taped to the door. Some were grainy, clearly screenshots. They were placed half-hazardly, covering the entire surface from top to bottom. In the center, one had a thick red circle drawn around the tattoo on my ankle, the one feature that made me distinct. My legs almost gave out beneath me. The police arrived quickly, took the prints, dusted the door, asked questions, and assured me they would file a report. When they said there were no fingerprints besides mine, something inside me twisted. The idea that someone had been inches away from the door without leaving anything behind felt unreal. The officers gave vague explanations about delivery mixups and pranks, though. One of them had a look on his face that made it clear he didn't believe his own words. I spent the next several days inside the apartment as much as possible. Clare slept on the couch to keep me from feeling alone. I opened my Only Fans page and deleted most of the content, setting everything to private. I avoided checking messages or notifications. But one night, curiosity took over. I remembered a website I had used months ago, one that allowed creators to send small anonymous gifts to their top subscribers. The platform claimed to hide all personal information. I opened the order history and my hands shook as I scrolled. Gentle steps had used the same billing address across dozens of gifts sent to different creators, all women. Some of their usernames were familiar. I reached out to one of them online, a creator from Brisbane. She told me she had also received White Lilies with her username card earlier that year. The details matched exactly. It became clear that this man, whoever he was, had been watching us all. I collected every screenshot, every strange detail, and sent everything to the police. Their response felt routine. The man was in his 30s, no criminal record, legally doing nothing wrong based on what they could see. They suggested I move apartments as a precaution. I packed that same night. Clare helped me carry boxes down the stairs. The hallway felt colder than usual. The empty surface of the door looked like it was still covered in photos. It took weeks before I slept without waking up at every creek or shift of the building. I deleted every account tied to that username and stopped using any platform involving deliveries or anonymous exchanges. Sometimes old subscribers tried to reach me through leftover email links asking where I had gone. Each message made a wave of nosia rise through me. Eventually, life settled into a new rhythm. I worked more hours at the cafe to compensate. Clare and I moved into separate places when her aunt offered her a room across town. I kept my new address private, giving it only to work and family. The fear never fully went away, but it dulled into something manageable, a shadow that hovered at the edge, but didn't swallow me whole. Last month, I received a call from Tessa. She had moved as well, changed everything she could. She said things had been calm until one evening, she returned home and found a single white lily placed gently against her door. No note, no card, just the flower leaning there, the petals soft and perfect. She told me she felt the same cold panic I once felt. The realization that distance and changes of a dress meant nothing to someone committed enough to keep searching. I listened to her breathing, steady but tight, the same rhythm I had lived with for months. I suggested she contact the police again, even though both of us knew what their response would be. After the call, I stared in my apartment and stared at my own door for a long time. It looked ordinary in the dim evening light, just a surface of wood and paint. But the memory of what had once been taped there hovered over it like an invisible layer. Every night before bed, I still check the door. Not because I expect to see another flower, another photo, or another package, but because I am afraid of the day when I stop checking. I never expected my life to revolve around a small ring light, a tripod, and the cheap white desk shoved into the corner of my room. It felt strange how quietly everything had changed, how easily I had slipped into a routine I barely understood. I only told two people I had started an only fans page, and even that felt like too many. What I hadn't admitted to anyone was the real reason I started. It wasn't curiosity or financial desperation. Not really. It was the attention. I had spent most of my adult life drifting behind other people unnoticed. A presence that blended into the background, but online, I wasn't invisible. The more people subscribed, the more I convinced myself I mattered. It was comforting in a way that scared me. Even then, I had picked a random theme at first. Nothing complicated, just simple photographs taken in natural lighting. Bare legs stretched across my bed, some close-ups of my hands and feet. Nothing explicit, nothing that felt hard to explain if I ever had to. My apartment was small, a two room space above a laundromat. The constant hum of machines below was something I got used to. A steady vibration through the floorboards that reminded me I wasn't alone, even when the place was dead quiet. Most of my time was spent inside. My job at the grocery store ended late, and the rest of my hours were spent editing, posting, cleaning, and trying to keep my life stable. Bills piled up quickly and I convinced myself that having a few hundred subscribers was harmless. The messages from strangers didn't bother me. I ignored most of them anyway, but one account stood out in the beginning because the username was nothing like the others. It was just a set of numbers, no profile picture, no posts, no bio, just a silent subscription that started on my second week and never expired. I tried to ignore it. Lots of people used anonymous accounts. Still, something about the pattern of their behavior unsettled me. They never interacted, never liked anything, never tipped, never sent a request, just lurked. It felt like being watched in a way that didn't make sense, like someone breathing quietly behind me while pretending they weren't there. I kept brushing it off, but by the third month, I started noticing more. Every time I posted, that account viewed everything within seconds. The refresh speed wasn't normal. They saw things before most of my other followers even knew new content existed. I convinced myself it was harmless until I started receiving messages on my private Instagram from an account I didn't recognize. They weren't threatening or explicit. They were simple lines describing things around my apartment that only someone physically nearby should have known. One message mentioned the crack in my bedroom wall shaped like a tilted V. Another described the mailbox downstairs that leaned to one side because the screws were rusting out. Little details that most people never noticed. Things I didn't even pay attention to until I read them type back to me. There were no profile photos, no posts, nothing that could hint who it was. The account looked brand new. I blocked it immediately, but another one popped up the next day repeating the same kind of information in the same rhythm. I told myself it was coincidence that maybe one of my neighbors had recognized the building from the background. The window behind my desk overlooked the alley, the pale brick visible behind me whenever I filmed. Someone local could have pieced it together. That seemed possible enough. I tried to relax, but no matter how many times I blocked the new accounts, more kept showing up. What finally pushed me toward panic was a message describing the pattern of scratches on my front door. The only way anyone would know about those was by standing right outside. I checked the peole that night over and over, sweating each time I stared into the hallways distorted view. Nobody was out there. Nothing looked disturbed. The building was quiet in a heavy, suffocating way that made everything feel more unreal. I tried to sleep, but I kept waking up to every tiny noise, unsure whether it came from the laundromat below or someone outside my apartment. I stopped posting for 2 weeks. My subscriber count dropped and messages poured in from people asking if I was still active. But none of that bothered me as much as walking home each night in the dark. I kept imagining someone trailing behind me, watching me from a car or hiding in the shadows of the alley that led to the back staircase. I avoided looking behind me because I wasn't sure what I expected to see. Nothing physical ever happened. No one tried to force my door open. No strange packages were delivered. Nothing dramatic unfolded. It was just a slow, creeping sense of being monitored. Some nights I stood at my window and stared across the alley, trying to figure out if anyone in the facing building could see inside my apartment. The windows were old and grimy, many covered by makeshift curtains or blinds half torn away. Any one of them could hide someone quietly staring back. Eventually, I reopened my Only Fans because bills didn't wait and fear wasn't something I could afford. I told myself I'd be more careful. I used tighter cropping in my photos, avoided showing my window, and tried to keep the background as plain as possible. For a little while, it seemed to work. I didn't receive any strange Instagram messages. The anonymous subscriber still lurked, but they didn't do anything noticeable. I let myself relax again. Then one evening while sorting laundry, I noticed something wedged under my front door. It was a folded piece of white paper. No writing on the outside. I stared at it for a long time, unable to bring myself to touch it. I checked the peole. The hallway was empty. My heartbeat felt too strong, like it was trying to climb out of my chest. I crouched down and finally picked it up. Inside was a printed screenshot of one of my Only Fans posts, one I had never shared anywhere else. It was a shot of my legs stretched out on my bed, but someone had circled a reflection in the metal pole of the lamp beside me. I could barely make it out, but the reflection looked like the outline of a person standing halfway in my bedroom doorway. I didn't remember anyone being there. I didn't remember anything unusual at the time I took the photo, but the circled shape made my stomach twist with a sharp cold fear I'd never felt before. I checked every corner of my apartment, even the closets, opening each door slowly while trying to steady my breathing. Nothing was out of place. No signs that anyone had ever stood in the spot the reflection seemed to show. I kept telling myself it was just a trick of the metal, something distorted or stretched by the curve. But the paper under my door wasn't an accident. Someone had been inside at some point. I just didn't know when I stopped sleeping again. Weeks passed where I woke up exhausted, jumping at small noises, unable to shake the feeling that the apartment no longer belonged only to me. I changed the locks. I installed a cheap camera facing the door. I checked the footage constantly. Nothing ever appeared, but the camera eventually started disconnecting at night, only at night, only when I was asleep. I finally reached a breaking point when I returned from a late shift and found the camera removed from the wall and placed gently on my bed untouched except for the fact that someone had held it. I didn't bother checking the footage. There was no reason left to. I packed everything I could carry into a duffel bag and left the apartment before sunrise, not telling anyone where I was going. I took a bus across the state to stay with a cousin I barely talked to. Someone who lived far enough away to make it feel like distance mattered. I deleted my Only Fans account the next morning. The anonymous subscriber had viewed my last post only minutes before I took it down. The same as always. I stared at that final notification until the page disappeared completely. It has been months since then. Nothing new has happened. No strange messages, no unexplained appearances, no signs that anyone has followed me. It's easy to pretend the fear was just stress and bad timing, that maybe someone in my old building had too much time on their hands. But sometimes at night when the house is quiet and I'm trying to fall asleep, I remember the reflection on the lamp and the fact that someone circled it for me. They wanted me to see it. They wanted me to know. I have replaced nearly everything from my old life, but I still own the lamp. It sits in the corner of my cousin's guest room, unplugged and unused. I can't bring myself to throw it away, even though I should. Some nights, I catch myself staring at its metal surface, looking for shapes that shouldn't exist, half expecting to see a figure standing behind me, waiting for me to notice.

Security & privacy tips from Nate Bartram: Kind: captions Language: en hey my name is Nate Bartram and I run the new oil which is a project dedicated to teaching about data privacy and cyber security and some simple easy ways that people can protect their own data as a content creator privacy is equally important to me it's important for me to be able to protect my identity and control who knows what about me so that I can avoid things like impersonation identity theft and stalking speaking of if you are any kind of a content creator one of the best ways to compartmentalize is to use an alias name for example my real name isn't actually Nate and I'm actually pretty open about that fact I'm using a fake name because like I said I want to protect my privacy too you can use a fake name on social media you can use a shortened version of your real name like Alex instead of Alexander in some Industries like Twitch streaming or YouTube it's okay to use handles and usernames like Markiplier or Mr Beast who are a couple of famous YouTubers when you do this it makes it really hard for cyber criminals to find useful information about you because they don't really have anything to start with and that actually touches on the concept of compartmentalization this is one of the best ways to protect your privacy and Security is to compartmentalize your life you want to share certain information with your co-workers but you want to withhold other information you're probably already doing this for example when you go to work you have a separate email account you probably dress a certain way that you don't normally dress at home and you may even interact with your bosses and co-workers in a different way than you do with your friends and family and this doesn't mean you're lying or being fake or anything like that it's just that you're choosing what you want to share and who with you can protect your privacy and security by doing this with your Digital Life some common examples are some people will use a separate browser for banking or for online shopping you might use a different email address for newsletters or social media than for your friends and your family how much you want to compartmentalize is completely up to you some people go really hardcore other people just separate work and personal either way adding that layer of separation is a really powerful way to protect yourself
